segments.s217-g329: که‌ای here is the vocative particle (classical/poetic 'که ای' = 'O'); it addresses someone directly. پهلوان جهان is a noun phrase in the order 'hero (پهلوان) of the world (جهان)' — the epithet follows the noun. شادباش is an optative/imperative formula 'be happy' or a greeting, directed at the addressed hero.
segments.s217-g330: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' (meaning 'free from' here). تیمار و درد و غم is a coordinated list 'care and pain and sorrow' where و links items. آزاد is an adjective 'free', and باش is the imperative 'be' — together آزاد باش = 'be free'. The whole is an imperative wish: 'be free from care, pain and sorrow.'
segments.s217-g331: The initial که is a coordinating/subordinating particle typical in classical Persian linking clauses ('that/when/then'). از راه ایران is a prepositional phrase 'from the road/way of Iran' (preposition + noun + genitive). یکی تیره گرد is a noun phrase 'a certain dark (tūre/tire) cloud/host' where یکی = 'a certain' and تیره = 'dark/ominous'; گرد here names a mass/rounding (poetic 'cloud'/'host').
segments.s217-g332: پدید آمد is a compound perfective 'appeared/ came into view' (پدید + آمد). و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. روز شد is 'the day became' (شد as intransitive 'became'). لاژورد is the predicative adjective/noun 'lapis-lazuli/azure' describing the day: 'the day became lapis-blue.'
segments.s217-g333: فراوان modifies درفش and functions adverbially/quantitatively: 'many/much' or 'in great number'. درفش 'banner' is the subject of برآمد in the next segment. از میان سپاه is a locative phrase 'from among the army/host' (preposition + noun + genitive), specifying origin.
segments.s217-g334: برآمد is the past tense 'rose/issued forth' and governs the following descriptive phrase. بکردار is an adjectival/qualifying element 'with/of good/noble bearing' that modifies the banner(s). تابنده ماه is a compound epithet 'shining (تابنده) moon (ماه)' — together describing the device/appearance of the banner as 'bearing a shining moon.'
segments.s217-g335: بپیش is an adverbial/locative 'forward/toward the front'. اندرون here means 'into the interior/midst' and indicates motion or placement. گرگ پیکر is a compound nominal phrase 'wolf-bodied' (گرگ = 'wolf', پیکر = 'figure/body'), describing the appearance of the person/figure. یکی is an indefinite 'one/a certain' introducing that individual.
segments.s217-g336: یکی again introduces another individual 'one/a certain'. ماه پیکر is a descriptive compound 'moon-bodied' (here idiomatically 'moon‑faced' = very beautiful/bright); note ماه in poetry often means a beautiful face. ز دور اندکی is a small prepositional phrase 'from a short distance' (ز = 'from', دور = 'far/distant', اندکی = 'a little'), indicating that this moon‑faced figure stood a little way off.
segments.s217-g417: نگویند is the negative hortative/subjunctive form (ن + گویند) meaning 'let them not say' or 'may they not say'; here it introduces what should not be spoken. بی‌نام modifies گردی and means 'nameless' or 'unsung'; in poetry an adjective precedes the noun. گردی is a poetic/figurative noun 'dust' used metonymically for a person; it takes no ezāfe here. بمرد is the subjunctive/optative form of مردن used as the content clause after نگویند ('that he die / has died'); classical Persian often uses this bare stem form.
segments.s217-g418: مگر introduces an exception/conditional meaning 'unless' or 'except that'. زیر is the preposition 'under/beneath' and خاکم is خاک + possessive suffix -م 'my earth/soil' (the suffix is enclitic). بباید is the impersonal/modal 'it must/one ought' (classical form with the b- prefix), followed by the bare verbal stem سپرد (literary infinitive/subjunctive) 'to consign/bury' — together meaning 'must be consigned/buried'. The clause is elliptical about the agent, a common poetic economy.
segments.s217-g419: بدین is a classical adverb/prepositional phrase 'by this / thus' (here written as ب + دین in the segmentation); it sets the circumstance. رام گشتند is a verb phrase with رام (tame/subdued) as predicate and گشتند the past plural verb 'they became' — literally 'they became tamed.' یکسر is an adverb/determiner 'entirely/wholly' placed before سپاه 'army/host', so یکسر سپاه = 'the whole army.' Note the poetic ordering (circumstance + predicate + subject).
segments.s217-g420: هرانکس 'whoever / every one' begins a relative clause introduced by که 'who/that'. بود is the past form 'was' (classical past tense). اندران is a classical locative compound meaning 'in/within that' or 'therein' (archaic use instead of modern 'در آن'), and رزمگاه is the noun 'battlefield.' The clause as a whole means 'whoever was in that battlefield,' with the relative clause modifying the preceding 'army' (i.e., every man who had been on that field).
segments.s217-g49: چو is the conditional 'if/when' introducing a hypothetical. بی‌رنج is a prefixed adjective (بی- 'without') meaning 'without trouble/effort' placed before the noun. دشمن بچنگ آیدت orders the elements as Topic (دشمن) + locative/adverbial phrase (بچنگ 'into the grasp') + verb آیدت 'comes to you / befalls you' where ت is the 2nd-person enclitic indicating the person affected.
segments.s217-g50: Another conditional clause introduced by چو 'if/when'. بشتابیش is an archaic/poetic verbal form meaning 'you hasten' (imperative/hortative sense in context), modifying the following result. کار is the noun 'affair/thing' and تنگ 'narrow/tight' is a predicate adjective; آیدت repeats the verbal form 'comes to you / befalls you' with the same 2nd-person enclitic.
segments.s217-g51: چرا is the interrogative 'why'. جست is an archaic perfect/preterital or infinitive-root meaning 'to leap/launch oneself' (here 'to rush'). باید is the modal 'must/ought', and همی is a poetic particle (intensifier = 'just/indeed') that emphasizes the verb; together they form 'one must not rush into battle' (or rhetorically 'why rush into battle?'). کارزار is the direct object 'battle/field'.
segments.s217-g52: طلایه 'vanguard' is the subject. بر + ین here functions as the fused prepositional phrase بر این 'on/over this' (بر = on/upon, ین = orthographic form of 'این' = 'this') modifying دشت 'plain'. بس is a limiting/adverbial particle ('enough/ample' or 'already') quantifying صد سوار 'a hundred horsemen' (سوار = horseman). Word order is subject + locative modifier + quantifier phrase.
segments.s217-g53: بباشیم is an optative/hortatory first-person-plural form 'let us be / let us remain' (poetic imperative sense). تا introduces purpose/result ('so that' / 'until'). دشمن از آب و نان places the affected party (the enemy) followed by a prepositional expression از + nouns 'from water and bread' meaning 'short of water and bread' (literally 'from water and bread').
segments.s217-g54: شود is the classical present/subjunctive of 'to become' (here 'becomes / will be'). تنگ is the predicate adjective 'narrow/strained' (i.e. 'in straits'). The particle زنهار is an interjection 'take heed / mark this' inserted before the following clause. خواهد is the future/modal 'will' and بجان is a fixed phrase meaning 'for/by life' or 'for one's life' (i.e. 'he will strive/plead for his life'). The sequence links result (shortage) to the consequence (he will seek his life).
segments.s217-g55: مگر introduces an exception or hypothetical 'unless / except'. خاک‌گر is a compound noun (literally 'earth-worker'); in context it names people driven to extremes (the exact occupational shade is archaic), placed before the object سنگ خارا 'flinty stone' (سنگ = stone, خارا = flint/hard rock). خورند is the classical present 'they eat' (3rd-person plural).
segments.s217-g56: چو is the temporal 'when/if'. روزی here is used in a poetic/archaic sense related to 'provision' or the coming of a day's sustenance (the supplied lemma senses do not include the common 'provision' sense, so this is lexically approximate). سرآید is 'comes/arrives' and خورند repeats 'they eat'. The final مرند in the printed line is problematic (it likely means 'they die' in sense, but the provided senses list gives a toponym); because the listed senses do not match the clear poetic meaning, its sense is left unspecified. Grammatically this clause says 'when their (day's) provision comes, they eat and ...' with the final verb completing the consequence.
segments.s217-g409: بسی گشته‌ام بر فراز و نشیب is a past perfective-style clause: بسی (an adverb 'much/many') modifies گشته‌ام ('I have been/been about' — here: 'I have gone/roamed'). The prepositional phrase بر فراز و نشیب ('over summit and descent') lists two opposites in the classical poetic order (fraz then nashīb) and marks the domain of the roaming. Word order is Subject (implicit) + predicate + locative phrase.
segments.s217-g410: برویم نیامد ازینسان نهیب is a nominal/verbal clause where نیامد ('did not come') is the main verb and نهیب ('a rebuke') is its object; ازینسان ('of/like this') qualifies the rebuke. برویم here functions as a nounal/possessive idea 'my/our appearing; my going' (an older/poetic use of a verbal form as a substantive), so the literal order is 'to my/our coming, such a rebuke did not come'. The negative verbal form نیامد is simple past negative; the whole clause states that such a reprimand had never occurred to the speaker in his comings.
segments.s217-g411: کنون چاره کار ایدر یکیست places temporal adverb کنون ('now') at the front, followed by a two‑noun compound چاره کار ('remedy/solution for the affair') and the locative adverb ایدر ('here'); یکیست ('is one') is the copula+predicate, stating there is a single course of action. The structure is adverb — noun phrase — locative — predicate (typical literary order).
segments.s217-g412: اگر چه سلیح و سپاه اندکیست is a concessive clause introduced by the compound اگر چه ('although'); سلیح و سپاه ('arms and army') are the coordinated subject(s), and اندکیست ('are few/there is little') is the predicate (note the archaic enclitic -ست forming 'is/are'). The phrase concedes weakness in matériel and numbers while not changing the previously stated single remedy.
segments.s217-g413: بسازیم و امشب شبیخون کنیم uses first-person plural hortative/subjunctive verbs بسازیم ('let us prepare/make') and کنیم ('let us do/make') joined by و. امشب ('tonight') is a temporal adverb modifying شبیخون کنیم ('let us do a night-raid'); the verbs are present/subjunctive/hortative in tone, giving an order or plan.
segments.s217-g414: زمین را ازیشان چو جیحون کنیم has زمین را as the definite object marked by را, from ایشان ('from/of them') indicates the source/target of the action, چو ('as/like') introduces a simile, and جیحون is the simile's term (the Amu Darya); کنيم is the hortative 'let us make/make so'. The whole means 'we will make the land of them like the Jihun', i.e., transform their land as if it were the Jihun (a poetic hyperbole).
segments.s217-g415: اگر کشته آییم در کارزار is a conditional clause: اگر ('if') introduces the condition; کشته آییم is a passive/resultative expression ('we are killed' / 'we should be killed'), where کشته (past participle 'slain') combines with آییم (subjunctive/present of آمد in a periphrastic sense) to express 'be killed'. در کارزار ('in battle') is a locative adjunct specifying where the killing would occur.
segments.s217-g416: نکوهش نیابیم از شهریار has نکوهش ('reproach') as the object and نیابیم as the negative-subjunctive/present form of یافتن ('find/receive') prefixed by ن- ('not'), here meaning 'we shall not receive/find'. از شهریار ('from the king') marks the agent/source of reproach. Together: 'we shall not receive reproach from the king.'
segments.s217-g33: نه ... اندرند is a negative statement about the host: نه at the start negates the following clause. لشکر is the subject ('army, host') and بدشت is a locative/goal meaning 'on the plain'; شکار is a predicate noun meaning 'prey/quarry' (they are in the state of being prey), and اندرند is the archaic present plural 'they are'. The word order places the negator + subject + locative + predicate before the verb 'are'.
segments.s217-g34: که introduces an explanatory subordinate clause ('for/that/because'). اسپان ما is the topic 'our horses' (اسپان = horses, plural) with the possessive pronoun ما. زیر بار is a prepositional/compound phrase 'under a load' (زیر = beneath; بار = load), and اندرند is again the present plural 'they are' — together meaning 'for our horses are under a load / are laden.'
segments.s217-g35: بدو is the prepositional phrase 'to him' (literally 'unto him') and stands before the verb in the older/poetic word order. گفت is the past of 'say' (here 'said'), followed by the subject پیران 'the elders' (inverted order: to-him said the elders). که introduces reported speech or a clause of content; تندی مکن is an imperative negative: تندی 'haste' as a noun and مکن 'do not' — literally 'do not (show) haste' = 'do not be hasty.'
segments.s217-g36: نه at the start negates the verbless copular clause that follows: روز شتابست = 'it is a day of haste' (شتابست is an archaic/persianized present-form 'is hasty'). So نه روز شتابست = 'this is not a day for haste.' و links the clauses; گاه سخن is a nominal phrase 'a time/occasion for speech' (گاه = time/occasion, سخن = speech), functioning as the contrastive predication to 'not a day for haste.'
segments.s217-g37: سه تن is a simple numeral + classifier phrase 'three persons/men' (تن = person). دوش here is an adverbial lit. 'last night' (common in classical Persian: 'دوش' = 'yesterday/last night'), so the time modifier follows the quantified noun. با + خوار مایه سپاه is an instrumental/associative phrase 'with the lowly/despised element/resource of the army' — خوار 'lowly/despised', مایه 'a contingent/substance/element', سپاه 'army/host'.
segments.s217-g38: برفتند is the past plural verb 'they departed/they went away.' بیگاه is an adverb 'at dawn' (or 'early'), specifying time of departure. زین رزمگاه is a genitive/locative phrase; in classical usage زین here functions as a demonstrative/deictic 'this' (though the provided sense list gives 'saddle' for زین, in this context it reads as 'this'), and رزمگاه 'battlefield.' So the clause means 'they departed at dawn from this battlefield.'
segments.s217-g39: چو is the comparative/simile marker 'like/as.' شیران جنگی pairs the noun شیران 'lions' with the adjective/epithet جنگی 'warlike/fighters' — together 'warlike lions' (figuratively: fierce warriors). و links the clauses; ما چون رمه is the counter-simile 'and we like a flock' (ما = we; چون = 'like'; رمه = flock/herd). Word order places the two similes in parallel.
segments.s217-g40: که introduces a relative/temporal clause 'that/when': 'that when/from which ...' از marks origin 'from/from out of.' کوهسار is 'mountainside/mountain highland.' اندر is a preposition/verb component 'into/within' used here with آید (آید = 'comes') — the verb phrase اندر آید is an archaic/periphrastic construction meaning 'comes in/comes forth.' دمه (literally 'breath' or 'moment') here is best read as 'a breath/gust' (a sudden blast); together the clause reads 'that a breath/gust comes from the mountains.'
segments.s217-g129: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' placed before its noun (سقلاب) as its object. چون + کندر is a simile 'like frankincense' that modifies the following nominal compound شیر مرد ('lion‑man'), where شیر (lion) + مرد (man) forms a heroic epithet 'lion‑hearted man'.
segments.s217-g130: چو introduces a temporal/conditional clause 'when/as', with پیروز as the nominal subject 'the victorious one'. کانی سپهر is a genitival phrase 'the mines/springs of the sky/heavens' (کانی modified by سپهر). نبرد is a poetic nominal/verbal element meaning 'battle' (here functioning as the action of contesting/waging battle).
segments.s217-g131: The opening چو signals comparison ('as'/'like') applied twice: first to سگسار غرچه (a compounded descriptive phrase in which سگسار is an epithet 'dog‑mauled / dishonoured' and غرچه is an obscure noun). The second چو begins a parallel simile 'like شنگل' with ز هند marking origin 'from India'.
segments.s217-g132: هوا پردرفش is an inverted predicative clause: هوا 'the air' + پردرفش 'bannered/covered with standards' (an adjective/epithet). و links the second predication, زمین پر پرند, where زمین 'the earth' is followed by the adjective phrase پر پرند 'full of birds' (پر used statively 'full (of)').
segments.s217-g133: چغانی is an adjectival or nisba form (likely a name or epithet) functioning as subject; چو + فرطوس is the simile 'like Fartus'; لشکر فروز is a noun + adjective 'the army/host luminous or glorious' where فروز qualifies لشکر.
segments.s217-g134: گهار گهانی is an adverbial/temporal phrase ('now and then', 'occasionally'), followed by گو used as a verbal/participial element 'saying/calling' that modifies گردسوز (here 'incense‑burner' or 'one who burns incense'), so the clause means that at intervals the incense‑bearers cried out or chanted.
segments.s217-g135: This is a coordination of epithets/items: شمیران (place/name) + شگنی (an epithet or type), coordinated with گردوی ('walnut' or 'walnut‑colored') and وهر (another archaic term). They form a list of things/epithets linked to what follows.
segments.s217-g136: پراگنده is a predicative adjective 'scattered/widespread'. بر نیزه و تیغ is a prepositional phrase 'upon spears and swords' (بر = 'on/upon'); زهر postmodifies تیغ as an attributive noun 'poison' or describes the swords as 'poisoned'. The whole phrase describes these things being scattered on spear‑points and poisoned blades.
segments.s217-g233: This is a compact clause with the prepositional pronoun + verb + subject: 'بدو گفت خاقان' = 'the khaghan said to him.' 'بدو' is the compound prepositional form (be + u) meaning 'to him/unto him' and comes before گفت. 'گفت' here functions as the past tense verb 'said' (classical narrative ordering places the object/indirect object before the verb). 'که نزدیک من' is a relative/quotative clause introducer 'that' + predicate 'near me' specifying what was said.
segments.s217-g234: This is a pair of coordinated imperatives expressing the khaghan’s command. 'بباش' is an imperative meaning 'be/remain' (here 'be near me'); 'و' links the two imperatives as 'and'; 'بیاور' is the second imperative 'bring'; 'یکی انجمن' is the object: 'one/ a certain assembly' (here 'an assembly'). Word order is Verb (imperative) + object, typical of Persian commands.
segments.s217-g235: 'یک امروز' = 'one today / for once today' — the numeral before the time phrase gives a sense 'at least for today' or 'once today.' 'با کام دل' is a prepositional phrase 'with the heart's satisfaction' (literal word order: with + satisfaction + heart) meaning 'to our heart's content.' 'می‌خوریم' is the present habitual/progressive first-person plural of خوردن ('we eat/drink'); the present prefix می- marks ongoing/habitual aspect.
segments.s217-g236: This is a hortative/negative-subjunctive construction: 'نشمریم' = ن + شمر + ایم (let us not count / we should not reckon). The phrase 'غم روز ناآمده' is a noun phrase 'the sorrow of a day not yet come' (روز + adjective ناآمده). The whole clause means 'let us not count/consider the sorrow of the day that has not come.'
segments.s217-g237: 'بیاراست' is a past/adaptive verb meaning 'adorned' or 'had adorned' and appears before its object (typical of Persian poetic order): 'بیاراست خیمه' = 'he adorned the tent' or 'he had the tent adorned.' 'چو' here means 'like/as' and introduces the comparison 'چو باغ بهار' = 'like a spring garden.'
segments.s217-g238: 'بهشتست' is a nominal predication 'it is paradise/heaven.' The following 'گفتی' is an archaic/modal use meaning 'one might say' or 'it could be said' (not simply the past tense 'you said'); it softens the assertion. 'برنگ و نگار' is a compound nominal phrase 'for colour (برنگ) and form/beauty (نگار)' — literally 'in colour and design,' giving reasons why one would call it paradise.
segments.s217-g239: 'چو' here introduces a temporal clause 'when.' 'بر گنبد چرخ' is a locative phrase 'over the dome of the sky' (preposition بر + noun phrase گنبد چرخ). 'رفت' is the past tense 'went/moved' and 'آفتاب' (sun) is the subject; word order is slightly inverted for poetic effect: 'when the sun went over the dome of the sky.'
segments.s217-g240: This main clause has the pattern Subject (دلِ طوس و گودرز) + predicate. 'دل' (heart) is the possessed noun, followed by the joint possessors 'طوس و گودرز' (Tous and Goudarz). 'شد' is the past of 'to become' ('became/was made'), and 'پر شتاب' is a predicative adjective phrase 'full of haste' (پر = 'full (of)'; شتاب = 'haste'). Together: 'the hearts of Tous and Goudarz became full of haste.'
segments.s217-g153: کنم is 1st-person singular present-subjunctive/volitive of کردن used here to express intention or a future/performative action (“I will do/give”). آفرین is a noun meaning an acclamation/praise ('praise, applause') and is the object of کنم. پیش + خاقان چین is a prepositional phrase 'in the presence of the Khaghan of China' (چین = China) — note the poetic order places the action-verb before its object and its circumstantial phrase.
segments.s217-g154: وگر is a conditional marker ('if' / 'or if') introducing an alternative. پیش تختش = 'before his throne' (تخت + possessive suffix -ش 'his'), where پیش means 'in the presence of/before'. ببوسم is again 1st-person singular present-subjunctive (here volitional/hypothetical) of بوسیدن 'to kiss', taking زمین 'the ground' as its object — an idiom for prostration.
segments.s217-g155: ببینم is 1st-person singular present-subjunctive/optative used here as a performative/desiderative 'let me see' / 'I will see'. سرافراز is an adjective 'proud/lofty' modifying the following name کاموس; را is the definite object marker, marking 'the proud Kamus' as the (definite) object of ببینم. Note poetic word order with the verb preceding its object.
segments.s217-g156: برابر here functions with کنم as a verbal complement meaning 'put/bring ... opposite' or 'make equal/face (against)'. کن م is the 1st-person verb 'I will make/put'. شنگل و طوس را is the coordinated direct-object phrase; in Persian the object marker را often attaches to the last coordinate to mark the whole phrase. طوس is the proper name Tūs (the hero).
segments.s217-g157: چو باز آیم is a temporal clause: چو = 'when', باز = 'again', آیم = 1st-person present-subjunctive/indicative 'I come' → 'when I come back'. ایدر = an archaic/poetic form of 'here'. ببندم is 1st-person present-subjunctive/volitive of بستن 'to close/bind' and takes an implied object (them); میان 'between' is an adverbial/postpositional element meaning 'in the midst / between', placed after the verb.
segments.s217-g158: برآرم is a compound verb (بر + آرم) in the 1st-person present-subjunctive meaning 'I will raise/bring forth'. دم و دود is a pair of nouns 'breath and smoke' functioning as the thing raised. از ایرانیان is a prepositional phrase 'from the Iranians' marking origin/agent — idiomatically 'to cause commotion/smoke to come from the Iranians.'
segments.s217-g159: اگر is the conditional 'if'. خود is the emphatic/reflexive 'themselves/their own'. ندارند is the 3rd-person plural present negative 'they do not have'. پایاب جنگ is a nominal phrase: پایاب (literally an irrigation/qanat outlet or productive outlet) modifies جنگ 'war' — used here metaphorically as 'a supply/footing/means for war'.
segments.s217-g160: بریشان is the fused sequence بر + ایشان meaning 'upon them' (بر = on/upon). کن م is 1st-person present-subjunctive 'I will do/make' (I'll bring upon them). روز تاریک و تنگ is the object phrase 'a dark and narrow day' (تاریک 'dark', تنگ 'narrow/tight') describing the misfortune inflicted — Persian places adjectives after the noun in this phrase.
segments.s217-g25: بیاورد is a past-tense/preterite verb 'brought' (classical order can put verb early). زان روی is a small classical phrase meaning 'for that reason / on that account' (زان = 'of/for that', روی here = 'face/occasion' used idiomatically). پیران سپاه is the noun phrase 'the elders' army' or 'the elders' (پیران) as the agents with سپاه 'army' as the thing brought; word order places the verb before the agents and object as is common in older Persian-poetic style.
segments.s217-g26: شد is the past copular 'became'. از گرد خورشید تابان سیاه is an adjectival/complement phrase explaining how they appeared: از + گرد (here 'dust' or 'particles') of خورشید 'the sun' (تابان 'shining') yields 'blackened by the dust of the shining sun' — سیاه is the predicative adjective 'black'. The whole phrase modifies the preceding noun (سپاه) via the copula شد.
segments.s217-g27: از introduces a cause or source: 'because of / from'. آواز گردان is a genitive phrase 'the cry/sound of the troop' (آواز = voice/cry, گردان = troop). و باران تیر is a coordinate phrase 'and a rain/shower of arrows' (باران used figuratively as 'shower'). The whole phrase is an adverbial cause for the main clause that follows.
segments.s217-g28: همی is an emphatic/continuous particle placed before the verb phrase. چشم خورشید is a possessive-like compound 'the sun's eye' (چشم 'eye', خورشید 'sun'). شد is the past copula 'became' and خیره is a predicative adjective 'dazzled/staring'. خیر here is a short poetic particle used after خیره for emphasis or modal colouring (archaic/poetic usage) rather than its common 'good' sense.
segments.s217-g29: دو لشکر is the subject 'two armies'. بروی اندر آورده روی is a compact, slightly repetitive poetic locative expression meaning 'brought face-to-face / brought up before one another': بروی (on/against the face), اندر (into/in), آورده (brought, past participle used predicatively). The final روی reiterates the facial/face-to-face sense for emphasis — a common poetic device.
segments.s217-g30: ز marks source/partitive 'from (among)'. گردان 'the troop' is the source group. نشد is the negative past of 'become' / 'prove to be' (here 'did not prove / there was not'). هیچ‌کس is the indefinite 'no one' (logical subject of نشد), and جنگجوی is the predicate noun 'a warrior' — so literal structure: 'From the troop no one became/was a warrior.'
segments.s217-g31: چنین گفت is the stereotyped 'thus said' formula (چنین = 'thus/so', گفت = 'said' in context though the provided senses list treats it as a noun). هومان is the speaker's name, and بپیران is the dative/prepositional phrase 'to the elders' (بـ + پیران). که introduces the subordinate clause 'that/that ...' which here begins the content of what Hooman said.
segments.s217-g32: همی before a verb gives an ongoing/emphatic nuance. جست here is intransitive 'sprang/leapt' used metaphorically — 'war sprang up / was imminent'. باید is the modal 'must/it is necessary'. چه جویی is an interrogative phrase 'what pursuit/what plan (to seek)' with جویی (from جست/جوی) meaning 'search/plan'. درنگ is the noun 'delay/pause' used elliptically in an imperative sense ('do not delay' is implied by context), a terse command suited to the poem's speech.
segments.s217-g193: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a subordinate clause. پیران is the subject 'the elders' and بنزدیک لشکر is an adverbial phrase 'near the army/host' placed before the verb; رسید is the past tense 'arrived/drew near'. The whole clause means 'when the elders came near the army.'
segments.s217-g194: در و دشت is a paired locative expression 'door (town/gate) and plain' meaning 'in town and on the plain.' از + سم اسپان functions partitive/particularizing with the negative verb (ندید) to mean 'not even the hoof of a horse'; سم is 'hoof' and اسپان 'horses.' ندید is the past negative 'did not see.'
segments.s217-g195: جهان 'the world' is the noun; پر here is the adjectival/partitive 'full (of)' that follows the noun (common in classical Persian: 'X پر Y' = 'X full of Y'). سراپرده و خیمه are the items filling the world: 'inner curtains/chambers and tents.' بود is the past copula 'was.'
segments.s217-g196: زده is a participial/adjectival use meaning 'tinged/affected' (i.e., 'dyed' or 'streaked with') and prefaces the list of colors that modify the tents/curtains. The following coordination gives the sequence of colors: 'red and yellow and purple and dark-blue.'
segments.s217-g197: ز here is the preposition 'from/of' indicating material or source. دیبای چینی 'Chinese brocade' is a compound where چینی qualifies دیبا. The coordination 'و از پرنیان' repeats the prepositional idea: 'and from parnian (silk).' These are materials of the banners/flags mentioned next.
segments.s217-g198: درفشی refers to a banner/standard (here the object made of the materials just named). ز هر پرده‌ای 'from/of every curtain' indicates distribution — 'one for each curtain' — and در میان means 'in the middle.' Together: 'a banner in the middle of each curtain.'
segments.s217-g199: فروماند is a past-state verb meaning 'was stricken/overcome' (here 'was cast down' or 'dismayed'). و links the result clause. زان 'from that' + کارش 'his affair/sight' indicates cause: 'from that (sight/affair).' آمد شگفت is an impersonal/result phrase 'amazement came (to him)' — i.e. 'he was filled with wonder.'
segments.s217-g200: بسی functions as an intensifier 'greatly/much.' با دل 'with (his) heart' is an ablative/instrumental phrase indicating the experiencer, اندیشه 'thought' is the agent of mental action, and اندر گرفت with گرفت in the stative/passive sense means 'was seized' or 'taken hold of.' So 'his heart was greatly seized by thought' (i.e. he was lost in deep thought).
segments.s217-g369: سواری (noun) is the agent 'a rider/horseman' placed before the verb; بمژده is an adverbial/adhesive phrase 'with good tidings' (بـ- + مژده) modifying the coming; بیامد is the simple past 'came'. ز پیش is a short prepositional/adverbial phrase 'from ahead' placed after the verb for emphasis — the whole unit = 'a rider came forward with tidings'.
segments.s217-g370: بگفت is the past narrative 'he said' introducing reported content; آن functions as a demonstrative/introductory particle 'that' (pointing to the report); کجا رفته is the embedded question/indirect clause 'where (he) had gone' with رفته as the perfect/past participle meaning 'gone'; بد کم و بیش is an idiomatic adverbial phrase modifying the report (here meaning 'roughly/more or less' — بد is archaic/adverbial in this collocation, followed by 'کم' 'few/little' and 'بیش' 'more').
segments.s217-g371: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; بشنید is past-tense 'heard' (here V before subject, a permitted poetic word order); هومان is the proper name placed after the verb; بخندید is the following past tense verb 'laughed', coordinated by و 'and' with گفت 'said' which introduces the speech clause.
segments.s217-g372: که introduces the content clause 'that ...' (what he said). شد here is a past/resultative 'became/has happened' marking a change of state; بی‌گمان is an intervening adverb 'undoubtedly' (for emphasis); بخت بیدار is a nominal phrase 'fortune (bakh­t) awake' with بیدار as predicate/adjectival complement to بخت; جفت ('pair') is an appositive/qualifying noun attached to بخت — overall: 'that, undoubtedly, fortune has become awake for the pair.'
segments.s217-g373: خروشی is a noun 'a shout/roar' (subject or exclamation); بشادی is an adverbial/qualifier 'in joy/joyfully' (بـ- + شادی); ازان is the prepositional 'from that' (poetic contraction of از آن) and رزمگاه is the noun 'battlefield' which is the source — literal order 'a shout, joyfully, from that battlefield.'
segments.s217-g374: بابر is the proper name/subject; اندر آمد is the compound verb 'entered/ came in' (اندر = in/among + آمد = came); ز توران سپاه is an ablative/genitive phrase 'the host/army from Turan' following the verb in inverted poetic order — the line presents Baber entering, specifying that he comes with the Turanian army.
segments.s217-g375: بزرگان ایران is the nominal subject 'the great ones/elders of Iran'; پر از is a predicative phrase 'full of' where پر (here 'full') takes an از + noun complement; داغ و درد are the nouns 'grief/burn (metaphorical sorrow) and pain/sorrow' coordinating the contents — together: 'the chiefs of Iran, full of grief and sorrow.'
segments.s217-g376: رخان is a poetic plural/collective referring to faces/cheeks (poetic use of رخ/رخان); زرد is the predicate adjective 'yellow/pale' describing those faces; و links the next clause 'and'; لبها is the plural 'the lips'; شده is the resultative 'have become/turned'; لاژورد is the color noun 'lapis‑lazuli/azure' — together: 'their faces pale and their lips have turned lapis‑blue.'
segments.s217-g1: شبی is an adverbial time phrase 'one night' placed at the clause start. داغ دل is a compound 'burning grief/ache of the heart' and پر ز تیمار طوس means 'full because of concern for Tūs' (ز marks the cause/reason and طوس is the proper name 'Tūs').
segments.s217-g2: بخواب اندر آمد is a poetic verbless+verb sequence meaning 'fell into sleep' or 'sleep came upon (him)'; بخواب here functions as ‘into sleep’. گه introduces a temporal clause 'when/at the time that', and زخم کوس is a genitive phrase 'the blow/beat (زخم used metaphorically) of the drum (کوس)'.
segments.s217-g3: چنان دید is 'he saw thus/so' (چنان...که correlative). روشن روانش is 'his bright/illuminated soul' (روان + enclitic -ش = 'his'), and بخواب states the condition 'in sleep' or 'at rest in sleep'.
segments.s217-g4: که introduces the result clause 'so ... that'. رخشنده شمعی is the noun phrase 'a radiant (رخشنده) candle (شمعی)', and برآمد ز آب is the past verb phrase 'arose/appeared from the water' (برآمد = third-person past).
segments.s217-g5: بر + شمع رخشان means 'upon the radiant candle'. یکی تخت عاج is an indefinite phrase 'a certain/one ivory throne'—یکی used here as an indefinite article-like word preceding تخت عاج.
segments.s217-g6: سیاوش is the subject; بران تخت = 'on that throne' (بران = thereon/thereupon). با فر و تاج is a prepositional phrase 'with (his) farr (royal glory) and crown' providing descriptive accompaniment. The clause is a verbless poetic description: Siavash is presented as seated/placed 'on that throne with glory and crown.'
segments.s217-g7: لبان پر ز خنده is a noun phrase 'lips full from laughter/smiles' (پر ز indicates 'full of/with'). زبان چرب‌گوی is another noun + adjective: 'a tongue that speaks smoothly' i.e. 'glib' or 'flattering'. The two noun phrases jointly describe the manner/appearance.
segments.s217-g8: سوی طوس کردی is a complete verbal phrase 'you turned toward Tūs' (کردی = second-person singular past). چو introduces a simile 'like/as', so چو خورشید روی means 'with a face like the sun' (خورشید = sun, روی = face).
segments.s217-g361: دگر باره (literally 'another time') is an adverbial phrase meaning 'again' or 'once more' and precedes the verb for emphasis. بنگر is the imperative form 'look' (poetic/older form of ببین), and ز کوه بلند is a prepositional phrase 'from the high mountain' where ز is the poetic preposition 'from' and بلند modifies کوه ('high mountain').
segments.s217-g362: که introduces a subordinate content clause ('that/when'), here marking an indirect question introduced by the interrogative کی ('when'). ایشان is the third‑person plural 'they'; بنزدیک ما is a locative phrase 'near us' (بنزدیک = near); کی precedes the verb in the classical/poetic order to mean 'when'; رسند is third‑person plural present/indicative (classical form) 'they arrive / will arrive'.
segments.s217-g363: چنین داد پاسخ is a fixed word order meaning 'gave such an answer' (چنین 'thus/so', داد 'gave' as the verb, پاسخ 'answer' as the object). که then introduces the reported content; فردا پگاه is a temporal adverbial 'tomorrow at dawn' (فردا 'tomorrow', پگاه 'dawn').
segments.s217-g364: بکوه هماون is a prepositional phrase 'to/at that mountain' (بکوه = 'to the mountain', هماون = 'that same/that very'). In poetic word order the verb رسد ('will reach/arrive') appears before its subject; آن سپاه that follows is the subject 'that army'. So the clause reads 'that army will arrive at that mountain.'
segments.s217-g365: چنان شاد شد is 'became so/that happy' (چنان 'so/thus' modifies شاد شد 'became happy'). زان سخن پهلوان is an ablative phrase 'from that speech (to the) hero' or more naturally 'the hero at that speech' — زان = 'from that/at that' and سخن پهلوان identifies the source of joy ('the hero's words' or 'to the hero'). Word order places the cause before the subject/verb in this poetic line.
segments.s217-g366: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. بیجان شده is a perfect/participial construction 'having become lifeless/unconscious' (بیجان 'lifeless', شده 'become'). باز یابد روان is the main action: باز ('again') + یابد (classical present/indicative or stative 'finds/recovers') + روان ('spirit' = consciousness). Poetic inversion places the verb before its object: 'his spirit is found/returned.'
segments.s217-g367: وزان ('and/from that') links this action to the prior event. روی here is best read as 'countenance/face' or 'appearance' used idiomatically ('because of that appearance/situation'); پیران بکردار is a noun phrase 'the elders of good conduct' (پیران 'elders', بکردار 'virtuous/well‑behaved'). گرد is the adverbial particle 'together/around' that will join with the verb of gathering in the next segment.
segments.s217-g368: همی راند is an imperfective/past iterative sense 'he/they drove (kept driving)' — همی is a continuous/iterative marker in classical Persian and راند is the past of راندن 'drove'. لشکر بدشت is the object plus destination 'the army to the plain' (بدشت = to/on the plain), and نبرد ('battle') qualifies the action: they drove the army onto the plain for battle. Subject is the elders mentioned in the previous segment; poetic word order places verb before object.
segments.s217-g377: باندرز کردن is a compound of the name/place باندرز plus the light verb کردن; here کردن turns the name into an action (’having gone to / having done Bandarz’) and functions adverbially to set the circumstance for the main clause. همه همگروه is the following noun phrase (’all the comrades’), which is the subject of the main verb in the next segment.
segments.s217-g378: پراگنده گشتند is a past plural predication: گشتند (from گشت#گرد) here means ‘became / were rendered’ and with the adjective/participle پراگنده yields ‘they were scattered’. بر گرد کوه is a locative phrase (بر گرد + noun) meaning ‘around the mountain’ and attaches to the verb as its place adjunct.
segments.s217-g379: بهر جای is a prepositional phrase meaning ‘to / for each place’; کرده is a perfect/participle (’having made / having set’) that with the following noun describes an accomplished action: یکی انجمن (‘one assembly / an assembly’). Together the phrase means ‘they made/held an assembly at each place’. Word order is typical classical Persian: prepositional phrase + participle + noun.
segments.s217-g380: همی is a classical durative/continuous marker (here indicating repeated or ongoing action). مویه کردند is the verb phrase ‘they lamented’ (مویه = lament as noun/gerund, کردند = did/made → ‘performed lamenting’). بر خویشتن is a reflexive locution meaning ‘about themselves / for themselves’, the object of their lament.
segments.s217-g381: که introduces a subordinate/clausal content (what they lament). زار is an adjective meaning ‘pitiable / afflicted’ modifying این دلیران (’these brave men’). خسرونژاد is a compound attributive meaning ‘of Khosrow’s lineage’ (a lineage-name attached to دلیران). The whole phrase is a noun phrase functioning as the object/content of their lament.
segments.s217-g382: کزیشان is the classical compound (کز + ایشان) meaning ‘of/from them’ or ‘concerning them’ and marks the source/topic. بایران here is a poetic/archaic locution meaning roughly ‘in Iran’ (a contracted or regional form used in epic diction). نگیرند یاد is a negative present/future sense ‘there will be no taking/receiving of remembrance’ → idiomatically ‘there will be no remembrance/they will not be remembered’. Grammatically, the verb نگیرند (3rd pl. negative) governs یاد (remembrance).
segments.s217-g383: کفنها is the plural subject (‘shrouds’). کنون is the temporal adverb ‘now’. کام شیران بود is a predicative construction: کام (desire/satisfaction) + شیران (of the lions/valiant ones) + بود (was) — ‘shrouds were the desire/lot of the warriors’. The verbless noun phrase is completed by the copula بود in past tense.
segments.s217-g384: زمین پر ز خون دلیران بود is a locative-descriptive clause: زمین پر (’the ground full’) with ز خون (’with/of blood’) specifying what fills it, دلیران is the genitive/possessive ‘of the brave’, and بود is the copula ‘was’. Word order places the locative noun phrase first, then the filling phrase, then the possessive, and finally the copula.
segments.s217-g209: بدو گفت = “he said to him”: بدو is the dative pronoun ‘to him’ placed before the verb (classical word order). گفت is the past verb ‘said’; بخ بخ is an interjection of admiration (repeated for effect). که introduces the content clause that follows, and با پهلوان ('with the champion') marks accompaniment and attaches syntactically to the following verb phrase.
segments.s217-g210: نشینم is first‑person singular present/subjunctive (‘I sit’ / ‘that I should sit’) used as the complement of the preceding که. چنین modifies the manner (‘thus’/‘so’). شاد و روشن‌روان are predicative adjectives describing the speaker’s state (‘merry and bright‑souled’); روشن‌روان is a poetic compound meaning cheerful/bright of soul.
segments.s217-g211: بپرسید is a past tense reporting verb (‘he/they asked’). زان پس (‘after that’/‘thereupon’) is an adverbial temporal phrase. کز (که + از) introduces the following nominal phrase in the sense ‘which of/from’, so کز ایران سپاه = ‘which of Iran’s host(s)’.
segments.s217-g212: که begins the relative/content clause ‘that/which’. دارد is the present indicative ‘has/possesses’ (3rd person). نگین و درفش و کلاه are the coordinated possessions (‘a gem, and a standard/banner, and a helmet/hat’) that the clause predicates of the host.
segments.s217-g213: کدامست is an interrogative ‘which/what is it?’. جنگی here functions as ‘warlike/fighter’ (describing the kind of force) and گردان is ‘troop/company’. کیند is an archaic literary form corresponding to کنند (‘they do’/‘they are’), so the clause questions what kind of fighting/troop they are.
segments.s217-g214: نشسته is a participle ‘seated/sitting’ describing the subjects. برین is the fused form of بر این (‘on this’), so برین کوه = ‘on this mountain’. سر بر چیند is a verb phrase in which سر ‘head’ is the object and چیند (= چیدن) ‘set/raise/put’ (3rd plural) describes their action: ‘they raise/hold up their heads.’
segments.s217-g215: چنین داد پاسخ = ‘thus gave answer/thus replied’: چنین (‘thus/so’) + داد (‘gave’, past verb) + پاسخ (‘answer’). بدو is the dative ‘to him’. پهلوان at the end is the subject ‘the champion’ placed after the verb and object—common in poetic/classical word order for emphasis.
segments.s217-g216: که introduces the quoted injunction. بیدار دل باش is an exhortation: بیدار (‘awake/alert’) modifies دل (‘heart’) and باش is the imperative ‘be!’. و روشن‌روان adds a second descriptive imperative complement (‘and be bright‑souled’), so the whole is ‘Be alert of heart and cheerful.’
segments.s217-g281: از ایران is a prepositional phrase (from Iran) using poetic ز/از; بیاید is the present/subjunctive form of آمدن used here in a narrative sense ('comes'/'may come'); همان (placed after the verb-phrase) emphasizes 'that very' or 'the same' announcement; آگهی is the noun 'announcement/news' and is the object/topic of بیاید.
segments.s217-g282: درخشان شود is adjective + شود (شدن in subjunctive/predictive) meaning 'become/shall be shining'; شاخ سرو سهی is a noun phrase 'the bough (شاخ) of the cypress (سرو) described by سهی' (a poetic epithet, here 'dark/black/tall'); word order places the predicate before the noun phrase that it predicates.
segments.s217-g283: سپهدار گودرز is the title + proper name ('Commander Goudarz'), with the title preceding the name as is common; بر تیغ کوه is a prepositional phrase using بر 'on/upon' and the compound تیغِ کوه 'the blade/edge (literally “sword”) of the mountain' = 'ridge/edge of the mountain' (poetic image).
segments.s217-g284: برآمد is a past/punctual verb meaning 'arose/appeared/emerged'; برفت is an older/formal past meaning 'went forth/advanced' (here successive action verbs); از میان گروه is a locative phrase 'out of/from among the group/troop' showing source. Note: برفت in the supplied senses list does not include the verbal sense 'went forth', so its sense is marked absent.
segments.s217-g285: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when/as'; خورشید تابان is 'the shining/radiant sun' (noun + adjective); ز گنبد بگشت = 'turned away from the dome/vault' where بگشت is the past of گشتن with the poetic prefix ب- (here 'turned'/'changed direction'). The verb b- form is an archaic/poetic past marker.
segments.s217-g286: ز بالا 'from on high/above' (ز used poetically); همی is a poetic particle indicating continuity/emphasis ('then/straightaway' or 'still') modifying the verb; سوی خاور گذشت = 'passed/moved toward the East' (سوی = toward, گذشت past of گذشتن = 'passed').
segments.s217-g287: بزاری modifies خروش and means 'with lamentation/mournfully' (an adjectival/adverbial form expressing wailing); خروش آمد is a noun + آمدن 'a cry/clamor came' (standard Persian order 'X آمد' = 'X came'); از دیده‌گاه is a source expression 'from the viewpoint/watch-tower/field of sight'.
segments.s217-g288: که introduces the subordinate content clause 'that ...'; شد is the past of شدن ('became'/'came to be'); کار گردان ایران is a genitive/possessive cluster where کار 'affair/work' is qualified by گردان (here functioning as 'governing/management' or 'those who turn/manage'); تباه is the predicate adjective 'ruined/corrupt'. The clause expresses the reason for the lament.
segments.s217-g41: Literally 'We found the whole plain full of streams of blood.' Here همه modifies دشت ('the whole/plain in its entirety'); پر+جوی+خون is a post-nominal phrase meaning 'full of streams of blood' (پر = 'full (of)'). یافتیم is the 1st-person-plural past tense 'we found.' Word order is poetic but essentially SOV with the verb final.
segments.s217-g42: Sentence: 'We found the heads of the renowned fallen.' سر ('head') takes the genitive نامداران ('of the renowned ones') and is modified by نگون ('fallen/overthrown'); یافتیم repeats the past 'we found.' نگون is an adjectival/postpositive modifier here describing the state of the heads.
segments.s217-g43: One possible reading: 'Some (places/people) have mountains, flinty and dry.' یکی is an indefinite 'a certain'/'some'; کوه is the noun 'mountain'; دارند is 3rd-person-plural present 'they have'; خارا و خشک are coordinated adjectives following the noun ('flinty and dry/barren'). The implied subject of دارند is indefinite/contextual (poetic ellipsis).
segments.s217-g44: Literally 'Their horses always smell thorns like musk.' همی is a classical prefix meaning habitual/continuous ('constantly' or 'still'); خار is the object 'thorn'; بویند is an older/poetic 3rd-plural form meaning 'they smell' (here 'perceive the smell'); اسپان is the subject 'horses' placed after the verb by poetic inversion; چو introduces a comparison 'like/as'; مشک = 'musk.'
segments.s217-g45: بمان is an imperative 'stay' or 'wait.' تا introduces a subordinate clause meaning 'until.' بران = 'upon them/thereon' (thence) or 'to those'; سنگ پیچان is a nominal phrase 'twisted/winding stones' with پیچان as an adjectival/participial modifier; شوند is 3rd-person-plural subjunctive/present 'they become' — together: 'stay until they become twisted stones.'
segments.s217-g46: چو introduces a temporal/comparative clause 'when.' بیچاره is an adjective/noun 'wretched/unfortunate'; گردند (classical gashte → 'become') and شوند ('become') are both verbs meaning 'become' — here a parallelism: 'when they are made/turned wretched, they become lifeless.' بیجان is the predicate adjective 'lifeless/dead.'
segments.s217-g47: گشاده ('open') is predicative; نباید is the impersonal/modal 'must not/should not.' که introduces the subordinate clause 'that ...' or functions to link the prohibition to what follows; دارید is 2nd-person-plural present 'you have/possess' and راه is the object 'way/path.' The structure: 'The way must not be left open that you have' → idiomatically 'You must not leave the way open.'
segments.s217-g48: دو رویه 'two-faced/two-sided' (literally 'two surfaces/faces') modifies the following locational phrase. پس و پیش is a fixed pair meaning 'behind and before' or 'back and front'; این رزمگاه specifies 'this battlefield.' Together: 'this battlefield should not have two fronts, back and front.'
segments.s217-g121: "چو" here is a simile meaning "like"; "منشور جنگی" is a noun phrase (منشور modified by the adjective/attributive جنگی) in poetic word order; the relative clause marker "که" begins a clause modifying منشور; "با تیغ اوی" is an instrumental prepositional phrase meaning "with his sword," where اوی is an archaic possessive form (= "his/her/its") attached after تیغ.
segments.s217-g122: "بخاک اندر آید" is a verb phrase meaning "comes/falls into the ground" with the directional prepositional element بخاک (to the ground) plus اندر (in/into) before the verb آید (comes/falls); note the poetic omission of an ezāfe between "سر" and "جنگجوی" — "سر جنگجوی" = "the head of the warrior."
segments.s217-g123: This is an equative phrase: "دلاور چو ... شمشیرزن" = "a hero like ... the swordsman." "چو" = "like/as," and "کاموس شمشیرزن" is a noun plus epithet (شمشیرزن modifies or apposes کاموس); there is no linking particle (ezāfe) because of the poetic register.
segments.s217-g124: The relative clause begins with "که" (= "who/which"); "چشمش" = "his eye" (چشم + enclitic -ش); "ندیدست" is an older/perfect form meaning "has not seen" (negative + perfect), so "چشمش ندیدست هرگز شکن" = "his eye has never seen a breaker" (i.e. never seen anyone who defeats/overthrows him).
segments.s217-g125: "همه کارهای شگرف آورد" = subject (همه کارهای "all the wondrous deeds") followed by آورد (past tense "brought/caused"); adjective شگرف follows the noun in poetic order. The implied subject (he) is omitted and understood from context.
segments.s217-g126: "چو" here is temporal = "when"; "خشم آورد" literally "brought anger" = idiomatically "when his anger/wrath rose"; the parallel clause "باد و برف آورد" repeats آورد to mean "he caused/brought wind and snow." The coordination places باد و برف as the direct objects of آورد.
segments.s217-g127: "چو" = "when" (conditional/temporal); "خشنود باشد" uses the subjunctive/optative form باشد after چو to mean "is/should be pleased"; word order is inverted (subject بهار follows the clause); آردت is the verb آرد "brings" with the enclitic -ت (= "to you").
segments.s217-g128: This continues the result: "گل و سنبل" (flowers and hyacinth) is the object, "جویبار" (stream/brook) is the agent placed before the verb, and again آردت = "brings to you." Poetic word order omits the ezāfe and repeats the verb with the enclitic to emphasize the giving to you.
segments.s217-g353: This is a compact clause: بدو (to him) is a prepositional clitic meaning 'to him/unto him' placed before the noun; دیده‌بان is the noun 'sentinel/watchman' (the speaker). گفت is the verb 'said' (past); کز introduces the quoted subordinate clause ('that/from which'); دیده‌گاه ('lookout/viewpoint') is the source referenced by کز. Word order is typical Persian: indirect object (بدو) + subject (دیده‌بان) + verb (گفت) + relative/introduction (کز) + noun.
segments.s217-g354: نشاید is an impersonal classical predicate meaning 'it is not fitting/appropriate'; شدن is the infinitive/verb 'to become' or 'to go' that completes the impersonal construction (i.e. 'it is not fitting that it should become/that they should advance'). پیش here marks relation toward/ in front of (the army being located 'before' or 'toward' Iran), followed by ایران سپاه (word order 'Iran army' = 'the army for/against Iran' or 'army toward Iran'). The whole string expresses an impersonal prohibition or ill‑fit about the army's movement.
segments.s217-g355: چو is a subordinating conjunction here meaning 'when'; بینم is 1st‑person present/past narrative 'I saw' (classical narrative often uses present forms); که introduces the subordinate content clause 'that ...'; روی زمین = 'the face/surface of the earth' (روی governing زمین); تار گشت uses گشت 'became' with تار 'dark' (poetic adjective) to say 'became dark'. The clause as a whole is a temporal clause ('When I saw that ...').
segments.s217-g356: برین is 'بر' + 'ین' (= 'on/at this' or 'at that'), so 'at that watchpoint'; دیده گه (دیده‌گاه) is 'watchpoint/lookout' and here the first دیده is 'eye' and گه is 'place/occasion' forming the compound; دیده بیکار گشت is a nominal phrase where دیده 'the eye/sight' + بیکار 'idle/useless' + گشت 'became' = 'the sight became idle/useless'. Word order places the locative/phrase 'برین دیده‌گاه' before the result clause.
segments.s217-g357: بکردار is an epithet 'virtuous/noble' modifying سیمرغ (the mythical bird), forming the subject. ازین (از + این) means 'from this/that', where ین is the orthographic form of این; دیده‌گاه is the locative 'lookout/viewpoint'. The order is epithet + name + source phrase: 'the virtuous Simurgh from that lookout...'.
segments.s217-g358: برم here functions as a verbal form meaning 'carried/took' (classical poetic form of بردن); آگهی is 'news/information' (the object carried); سوی + ایران سپاه marks direction 'toward the army of Iran' (سوی = toward, followed by the compound ایران سپاه). The clause thus states the action performed by the Simurgh: carrying word to the Iranian army.
segments.s217-g359: چنین 'thus/so' modifies گفت 'said' (here a past narrative verb). با + دیده‌بان پهلوان is 'to/with the sentinel, the champion' — با marks accompaniment or address and پهلوان apposes دیده‌بان (the addressee described as a champion). Word order is adverbial (thus) + verb (said) + prepositional phrase (to the sentinel, the champion).
segments.s217-g360: که introduces the content of what was said. اکنون 'now' sets time. نگه کن is an imperative composed of نگه 'watch/gaze' + کن 'do' (imperative 'keep/watch!'). بروشن روان is an adverbial phrase: بروشن 'brightly/clearly' modifies روان, where روان (here adverbial) suggests moving/flowing — together giving an exhortation 'go forth brightly/clearly' or 'proceed in bright/clear fashion'.
segments.s217-g393: ازان کوه سر کرد is an SOV/verb-cluster sequence where ازان کوه (from that mountain) is a prepositional/locative phrase and سر کرد is a compound verbal idea: سر (literally 'head') + کرد (past 'did') = 'raised (his) head' or 'looked up'. هر سو (literally 'each side') modifies نگاه ('gaze') and the phrase order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect: 'he raised his head and gazed in every direction.'
segments.s217-g394: This is a nominal list serving as the object/scene of his gaze. Persian omits a linking verb here; the items are coordinated with و. Read as 'the standard (banner), the riders, the elephants, and the army.' Each noun stands in apposition to what he sees.
segments.s217-g395: بیامد is the simple past 'he came'. بسوی is a preposition 'toward' and سپهبد is its object ('the spahbed' = general). دوان is a present-participle/adverbial form (from دواندن/دویدن) placed after the noun to indicate manner: 'came running' or 'came in haste toward the spahbed.'
segments.s217-g396: This is a cluster of adjectival/nominal phrases describing his inner state. دل (heart) is the theme; از غم (lit. 'from grief') gives the cause or content of the heart, with از used to indicate source/cause. پر از درد is a common construction 'full of pain' (پر = 'full', followed by از + noun). و خسته روان joins two predicates/adjectives: خسته ('weary') and روان (here 'spirit/soul'), both qualifying دل.
segments.s217-g397: بدو گفت is a dative + verb: بدو = 'to him' (indirect object), گفت = past 'said' (here used as verb). چندان is an intensifier meaning 'so great/to that extent' and is modifying سپاهست, where سپاهست is an archaic fused form meaning 'it is a host/army' (سپاه + -ست 'is'). و پیل simply adds 'and elephants' to the statement.
segments.s217-g398: که introduces a result/clause ('that/so that'). روی زمین گشت is 'the face/surface of the earth became/was turned' — گشت is the past of گشتن 'to become/turn/reach'. برسان نیل functions as a limit phrase 'as far as the Nile' (برسان here conveys reaching/up to), so the clause means 'so much that they reached/covered the earth up to the Nile.'
segments.s217-g399: درفش و سنان را uses را to topicalize/mark these items ('as for the banners and spears'). خود is emphatic 'themselves'/'indeed', and اندازه نیست is an idiomatic negative: 'there is no measure/limit' — i.e. they cannot be measured or counted.
segments.s217-g400: خور is 'the sun' as subject. از گرد ('because of/from the dust') gives the cause; بر آسمان is the locative 'on/in the sky'. تازه نیست literally 'is not fresh/new' here idiomatically means 'is not clear/visible' (dust obscures it). Together: 'the sun is not visible/clear in the sky because of the dust.'
segments.s217-g305: ازین زندگانی = از + این (written here as ازین) + زندگانی. The preposition از ('from, out of') precedes the demonstrative 'این' fused as archaic/poetic ازین, so this phrase means 'from this life'. شدم ناامید is a past-tense perfective: شدم (become/I became) + ناامید (hopeless), so the speaker states a completed change of state — 'I became hopeless.'
segments.s217-g306: سیه شد مرا بخت و روز سپید — سیه شد is a stative/result: 'became black/dark.' مرا را here marks the experiencer/indirect object ('for me' / 'to me'); in the manuscript the pronoun is split (م + را). The coordination 'بخت و روز سپید' is the grammatical subject that has 'turned dark' — 'my fortune and the bright day became black.'
segments.s217-g307: This is an exclamatory/wishal construction with کاشکی 'if only / would that'. نزادی (past of زادن) means 'had borne / given birth to' and is used here in the sense 'would that she had not borne me.' مرا is the object 'me' (split in the word list), and مادرم (my mother) is the agent — word order is poetic and inverted: 'کاشکی مادرم نزادی مرا' → 'I wish my mother had not borne me.'
segments.s217-g308: نگشتی سپهر بلند از برم — نگشتی is an aorist/past form of گشتن with the sense 'turned' (here 'turned away'); سپهر بلند 'the lofty/heavenly sky' is the subject; از برم ('from upon me' / 'away from me') uses از + بر + pronominal clitic -م. The clause says the heavens 'turned away from me' (result of my misfortune), so نگشتی is a change-of-state verb governing the rest.
segments.s217-g309: چنین گفت با دیده‌بان پهلوان — چنین 'thus/so' modifies the speech; گفت here is the verb 'said/spoke' (the word list gives noun senses, but in context it is the past-tense verb). با introduces the addressee 'دیده‌بان پهلوان' (to the sentinel/observer, who is a champion/hero). Word order: verb + prepositional phrase marking the interlocutor.
segments.s217-g310: که‌ای مرد بینا و روشن‌روان — که‌ای is an archaic/vocative formula (که + ای) equivalent to 'O' or introducing direct address; مرد 'man' is the vocative noun; بینا 'sighted/discerning' and روشن‌روان 'bright‑soul / clear‑minded' are epithets joined by و. The phrase is direct address: 'O discerning, bright‑soul man.'
segments.s217-g311: نگه کن بتوران و ایران سپاه — نگه کن is an imperative: نگه (watch/guard) + کن (do) → 'keep watch' or 'look!' بتوران و ایران سپاه = 'the Turanian and Iranian host/armies' with the place/name بتوران ('to/among the Turanians') followed by و and ایران سپاه; the order is poetic but means 'hold watch on the armies of Turan and Iran.'
segments.s217-g312: که آرام دارند از آوردگاه — که introduces a clause of explanation: 'for/that' (they are) آرام دارند 'they are calm/resting' (دارند is present plural 'they have/are in the state of'); از آوردگاه 'away/from the battlefield' gives the place from which they are removed. So the clause explains why a watch is needed: the armies are resting off the field.
segments.s217-g249: مگر functions here as 'unless' (introducing the exception/condition). The clause has subject رستم followed by the present-tense verb آید ('comes'); بدین is the fused preposition phrase به + این ('to this') and رزمگاه is the place 'battlefield'. Word order is: condition marker + subject + verb + goal phrase.
segments.s217-g250: وگرنه = 'otherwise'. بد آید is a verb phrase meaning 'ill/bad will come' (impersonal phrasing: trouble will befall). بما is the dative 'to us', and زین سپاه ('from this army') expresses the source of the harm; note زین here is orthographically fused (ز + این) and functions as 'from this', not the lexical 'saddle'.
segments.s217-g251: نیابیم is the first-person-plural negative/resultative form of یابیدن 'to find' (here 'we do not / cannot find'). ستودان functions as the object idea 'one to praise' (from ستودن), and یک تن 'a single person' emphasizes 'not even one'; نه گور = 'nor a grave'. The clause expresses inability to find any worthy person or even a burial-place.
segments.s217-g252: بکوبندمان is a third-person plural verbal form plus the enclitic object -مان ('them/they beat us' / 'they pound us'); the verb is presented as what will happen to us. سر is 'head' (the direct object), بنعل is the phrase 'under the horseshoe' (ب + نعل) and ستور is 'steed/horse' — together 'our heads under the horses' horseshoes'.
segments.s217-g253: بدو = به + او 'to him' (dative: 'he said to him'). گفت is the speech verb 'said' (here given in past narrative); گیو‌ای is vocative/addressing Giv (گیو + ای), and سپهدار شاه is a title apposition 'commander of the king'. The order is dative clitic/adverbial + speech-verb + vocative/title.
segments.s217-g254: چه introduces the question 'what'. بودت is the past/copular form with the second-person enclitic -ت ('was to you' / 'what was it'), here functioning idiomatically ('what possessed you' / 'what was the matter that...'). که introduces the subordinate clause 'that', اندیشه is 'thought/intention', کردی is second-person past 'you did' or 'you thought/acted', and تباه characterizes the action as 'ruinous/ruined'. The whole line asks why he acted so as to bring destruction.
segments.s217-g255: از introduces the domain 'from' or 'as for' and combines with اندیشه ما 'our thought/view'. سخن دیگرست is a predicative phrase meaning 'the matter/speech is different' — دیگرست is the copula-like adjective 'is different'. The phrase says 'as for our thinking, that is another matter.'
segments.s217-g256: ترا is the beneficiary/object 'to you' formed of the 2nd-person suffix ت plus را (here written as two tokens); کردگار جهان is an epithet 'the Maker/Creator of the world', and یاورست is a copular/adjectival phrase 'is a helper/ally'. Together: 'The Creator of the world is near/your helper.'
segments.s217-g9: The clause begins with که as a clause-introducer; ایرانیان را is the definite object (را marks the object), and هم adds 'also'. ایدر is the archaic/poetic word for 'here'. بدار is an imperative form meaning 'hold/keep' (classical imperative; addressed to someone and commanding that the Iranians be kept here). This clause sets up the action that serves the purpose given in the following clause.
segments.s217-g10: که here introduces the purpose/result clause 'so that...'. پیروزگر is an adjectival/nominative description 'victorious/triumphant' and باشی is the 2nd-person subjunctive/jussive (classical Persian form used like an exhortation or wish: 'may you be / be'); از کارزار literally uses از + noun to mean 'in/through the battle' (poetic prepositional use). The whole clause expresses the intended/resultant state.
segments.s217-g11: بگو is the imperative 'say' (addressing the speaker to tell someone). در زیان is a prepositional phrase 'about/in regard to loss' (در + زیان). هیچ غمگین مشو is a negated jussive: هیچ 'not at all' + غمگین 'sad' + مشو 'do not become' (negative imperative of شدن). The clause tells someone not to be saddened by loss.
segments.s217-g12: که here gives the reason/explanation ('for/because'): ایدر = 'here' (poetic), یکی 'one/a single' (classical usage for 'a' or 'one'), گلستانست is a fused noun+copula meaning 'is a rose-garden', and نو 'new'. Together this explains why one should not be sad: there is a new rose-garden here.
segments.s217-g13: بزیر گل اندر = 'beneath/under the flowers' (بزیر = under; گل = flowers; اندر = in/among — poetic order). همی is the older progressive/emphatic particle meaning 'still/continually', and می‌خوریم is the 1st-person plural present (here habitual/ongoing) of خوردن 'to eat/drink' (in a wine context it means 'drink'). The phrase describes ongoing revelry under the blossoms.
segments.s217-g14: چه دانیم = 'what do we know' or 'who knows' (rhetorical). کین here is an archaic/dialectal form used as a demonstrative 'this' (classical Persian), introducing 'this wine' (باده). تا کی = 'until when' (تا = until; کی = when), and خوریم is the 1st-person plural present/subjunctive of خوردن 'we drink'. The clause questions the length of their enjoyment.
segments.s217-g15: ز خواب اندر آمد شده is a compact poetic sequence: ز (from) خواب (sleep) اندر آمد (came out/in) شده (has become) — classical word order places verbs after modifiers. شاد دل = 'joyful heart' (شاد modifies دل). Grammatically, the verbs are perfective or resultative forms indicating that out of sleep the heart has become joyful.
segments.s217-g16: ز درد و غمان = 'from pain and sorrows' (ز = from; درد = pain/sorrow; غمان = sorrows). گشته is the perfect/participial form of شدن meaning 'has become/turned', آزاد modifies دل as 'free' or 'liberated', and دل 'heart'. The clause states the heart has been freed from grief.
segments.s217-g169: سه بهره (ordinal/quantifier + noun) = 'three shares/contingents.' ازان is the archaic compound of از + آن meaning 'from them/ from those.' پس here functions as 'then' (logical/temporal link). برانم is the first-person present/subjunctive/future form of راندن ('to drive, to lead'), taking سپاه ('army, host') as its object that follows the verb in this poetic word order.
segments.s217-g170: کنم is the first-person present/subjunctive of کردن 'to make/do' (here 'I will make' or 'I will bring about'). روز is the direct object 'a day.' بر marks the target of that action ('upon/over'), and شاه ایران is the target noun phrase 'the Shah of Iran.' سیاه is a postpositive adjective modifying روز (poetic order), yielding 'a dark/black day upon the Shah of Iran.'
segments.s217-g171: یکی بهره = 'one share/one contingent' (numeric determiner + noun). زیشان is the fused poetic form of از + ایشان = 'from them.' فرستم is the first-person present/subjunctive/future of فرستادن 'to send.' ببلخ is the prepositional adverbial 'to/at Balkh' formed with the locative prefix ب- attached to the place name.
segments.s217-g172: بایرانیان here names the target group 'the Iranians' (functioning with بر to show upon whom the action falls). بر کن́م روز تلخ = 'I will make/bring a bitter day' — کن́م (کنم) 'I will make,' روز 'day' and تلخ the postpositive adjective 'bitter/harsh.' The sequence places the target noun (بایرانیان) before بر and the verb, a common poetic inversion meaning 'I will bring a bitter day upon the Iranians.'
segments.s217-g173: دگر بهره = 'another/other share' (دگر = 'other/another' + بهره = 'share/contingent'). بر سوی کابلستان is a directional prepositional phrase ('toward Kabulistan'), where بر سوی functions together to indicate movement/direction toward the named region.
segments.s217-g174: بکابل is the locative/directional form 'to Kabul' (prepositional prefix ب- + place). کشم is the first-person present/subjunctive/future of کشیدن 'to pull/drag/bring' (in this poetic context 'I will bring/drag'), taking خاک زابلستان ('the earth/soil of Zabulistan') as its object; خاک is the noun 'earth/soil' and زابلستان specifies its origin. Word order places the direction (بکابل) before the verb for emphasis.
segments.s217-g175: سوم بهره = 'the third share/contingent' (ordinal + noun). بر سوی ایران = 'toward Iran' (direction). برم is the first-person present/subjunctive/future of بردن ('to take/carry/send'), here 'I will send/carry' the third contingent toward Iran.
segments.s217-g176: ز is the poetic preposition 'from.' ترکان names the source 'the Turks.' بزرگان و شیران are coordinated nouns 'the great ones and the lions' — شیران is metaphorical for brave warriors. The final برم repeats the verb 'I will take/carry' (first-person) and ties the action back to the previous clause: 'I will take it from the Turks, the nobles and the brave.'
segments.s217-g17: بگودرز گفت is a reporting clause: the proper name بگودرز (Goudarz) precedes the verb گفت (said), which is the past tense reporting verb. ای جهان‌پهلوان is a vocative phrase (ای + title) addressing the addressee directly: ای marks the call and جهان‌پهلوان is a laudatory title.
segments.s217-g18: یکی خواب دیدم is a compact clause where خواب دیدن = “to dream” functions like an object–verb sequence (‘I had a dream’). یکی (a certain) prefaces خواب to mean ‘I had a certain/a dream.’ بروشن روان is an adjectival phrase (ب + روشن modifying روان) meaning ‘bright/clear of spirit’ that describes the quality of the dream; روان (soul/spirit) is qualified by بروشن.
segments.s217-g19: نگه کن is an imperative phrase meaning ‘keep watch’ or ‘look!’ (نگه = watch/gaze, کن = do! imperative). که introduces the content clause that follows ('that' / ‘for’). چو is a comparative particle ‘as/like,’ linking رستم to the simile باد دمان; here رستم is compared to a violent gust (باد دمان).
segments.s217-g20: بیاید is an optative/irrealis form used here for future sense (‘will come’ / ‘may come’). بر ما places the action upon the speaker (‘upon us’). زمان تا زمان is a temporal phrase ('time after time' / repeatedly); زمان is ‘time,’ and the repetition with تا gives the sense of successive periods.
segments.s217-g21: بفرمود is a polite/honorific past reporting verb often used for commands by a lord (‘he commanded/said’). تا introduces a purpose or dependent clause ('that/so that'), followed by برکشیدند نای: برکشیدند is a past plural verb ‘they raised/lifted,’ and نای (ney) is its object — ‘they raised the flute’ (a war signal).
segments.s217-g22: بجنبید is an imperative/plural past‑root form used narratively to mean ‘they moved/stirred’ (in older Persian narratives imperatives or jussives appear in reported action). بر کوه places the motion onto the mountain (‘onto the mountain’). لشکر ز جای is an ablative phrase with ز = ‘from,’ so literally ‘the host from its place’ — together indicating the army left its station and moved onto the mountain.
segments.s217-g23: ببستند is a past plural verb meaning ‘they closed/formed’ (here: formed ranks). گردان ایران is a noun phrase ‘the troop/unit of Iran’ (گردان governing ایران), and میان indicates position ‘in the middle/center.’ Word order places the verb before its object in classical narrative style.
segments.s217-g24: برافراختند is a past plural verb ‘they raised/erected.’ اختر کاویان is a possessive/construct phrase: اختر (‘star’ / bright sign) qualified by کاویان (the Kayanian dynasty/kings), here referring to the royal standard or emblem they raised.
segments.s217-g273: به is the instrumental/prepositional particle 'by/through' here; پرهیز is a noun 'caution/forbearance' and is coordinated with اندیشه 'thought/counsel' by و; نابکار is an adjective following and modifying اندیشه ('wicked/evil thought'). The whole phrase names the means or agents ('by caution and wicked thought').
segments.s217-g274: نه negates the verb that follows; برگردد is a 3rd-person singular present/subjunctive of برگشتن used here in the sense 'turn away / turn back'; از ما is a prepositional phrase 'from us'; بد روزگار is the noun phrase 'bad fortune / unfortunate times' serving as the thing that will not be turned away. Word order places the verb before the noun phrase for emphasis.
segments.s217-g275: یکی is an indefinite/indicating 'one / a certain'; کنده is a participle/adjective 'uprooted, dug up' modifying an implicit noun; سازیم is 1st-person-plural present/subjunctive/hortative of ساختن 'we shall/may make'; پیش سپاه is a locative phrase 'in front of / before the army.' The verb سازیم follows the indefinite object, making a hortatory statement about an action the speakers will perform.
segments.s217-g276: چنانچون is a comparative/subordinating adverb 'just as, in the same way that'; بود is the past copula 'was' (here linking to the following nouns); رسم و آیین و راه are coordinated nouns ('custom / rite / way') that serve as the standard or pattern referred to by چنانچون. The clause means 'just as custom/ritual/way was.'
segments.s217-g277: همه جنگ را places 'the whole war/battle' as a definite object marked by را; تیغها is the subject 'the swords' (plural); برکشیم is 1st-person-plural hortative/subjunctive of برکشیدن meaning 'let us draw/raise (our swords)'. Persian permits the object phrase to precede the subject and verb for emphasis: 'we should draw the swords for the whole battle.'
segments.s217-g278: دو روز دگر is a temporal phrase 'two more days'; ار is the conditional particle 'if / supposing'; کشند is 3rd-person-plural subjunctive (they) and کشیم is 1st-person-plural subjunctive (we) of the verb کشیدن here meaning 'to draw (weapons)' — the line sets up a conditional contrast 'if they draw, or if we draw.'
segments.s217-g279: ببینیم is 1st-person-plural hortative/subjunctive 'let us see'; تا here introduces an indirect interrogative clause ('what') linking to چی 'what' and ست (ست = است) as the copula 'is'; آغازشان is the possessed noun 'their beginning'. Together it means 'let us see what their beginning is.'
segments.s217-g280: برهنه is an adjective 'exposed / bare'; شود is the 3rd-person-singular present/subjunctive/passive of شدن 'become / be' used to form a passive ('be exposed'); بی‌گمان is an adverb 'undoubtedly'; رازشان is the possessed noun 'their secret.' The clause reads 'their secret will be exposed without doubt.'
segments.s217-g297: چنین گفت is a fixed order where the adverb چنین ('thus') precedes the verb گفت ('said') meaning 'he said thus'. کز introduces a subordinate clause ('that / from') and connects to the following noun phrase; اختر روزگار is a poetic genitive: 'the star (اختر) of fate (روزگار)' with no ezāfe explicitly written.
segments.s217-g298: مرا here functions as 'to me' (the sequence is split in the annotation as م + را). بهره کین آمد is the core predication: بهره ('a share') qualified by the following noun کین ('hatred/vengeance'); آمد is the past tense verb 'came'. The coordinating و links the additional nominal کارزار ('battle'), so the clause means 'a share of hatred and battle came to me.'
segments.s217-g299: ز گیتی is a prepositional phrase 'from the world' or 'because of the world'. مرا (annotated as م + را) marks the experiencer 'to me'. شور بختیست is a nominal predicate with the copula fused into بختیست ('it is fate') meaning 'a tumultuous/turbulent fortune it is (for me)'. بهر here functions as a linking preposition 'for' pointing ahead to what follows.
segments.s217-g300: This clause has the adjective پراگنده ('scattered/widespread') with a locative phrase بر جای ('upon the place') followed by the compound noun تریاک زهر ('opium-poison' = poisonous opium). The verb 'to be' is omitted (typical in Persian poetry), so the phrase reads 'opium-poison is scattered upon the place.'
segments.s217-g301: نبیره پسر is a noun phrase meaning 'a scion/offspring son' (نبیره qualifying پسر). داشتم is the past tense 'I had'. لشکری follows as an attributive noun/adjective 'military' or 'of the army', describing the son (literary omission of ezāfe is common).
segments.s217-g302: شده is the perfect/participial form 'has become' or 'became' and combines with the adjective نامبردار ('renowned, famous'); هر کشوری is a quantifying locution 'in every country/realm' (هر = 'every', کشوری = 'of the country/realm'). Together: 'became renowned in every land.'
segments.s217-g303: بکین is an archaic/poetic formation meaning 'with/by vengeance/hatred' (ب + کین), introducing the cause or instrument. The clause SOV order places سیاوش (Siyâvash) before the passive predicate همه کشته شد 'was all killed' (همه = 'all, entirely'; کشته شد = 'was killed').
segments.s217-g304: ز من is 'from me' (indicating that something left me). بخت بیدار is a compound 'wakeful/alert fortune' (bēdar used adjectivally). برگشته شد is the verbal phrase 'turned back/returned' but here idiomatically means the fortune turned away or departed; the auxiliary/copeula شد marks the change of state.
segments.s217-g385: سپهدار is the title/agent 'the commander' (noun) placed at the start for emphasis. با + names (بیژن گیو) indicates with/to whom he spoke ('to Bijan and Giv'); گفت is the reporting verb 'said' (here used to introduce quoted/reported speech). Word order is somewhat topical (agent → indirect object → verb) as common in epic/poetic Persian.
segments.s217-g386: که introduces the reported command ('that, saying'); برخیز and بگشای are imperatives ('stand up' and 'open/reveal') formed without an explicit pronoun, the prefixed b- in بگشای is the classical/poetic present/imperative marker. راز از نهفت is the object phrase 'the secret from/hidden [place]'—از links راز to نهفت (hiddenness), so the sense is 'reveal the secret that is concealed.'
segments.s217-g387: برو is an imperative 'go'. تا here marks the limit/goal 'up to/until', and the following nominal cluster 'سر تیغ کوه بلند' is the destination: سر = 'summit/top', تیغ = 'point/ridge' (poetic image), کوه = 'mountain', بلند = 'high'. In poetry the ezāfe linking is often omitted, so nouns stack to form 'the summit of the mountain's ridge, high.'
segments.s217-g388: ببین is the imperative 'see' (i.e., 'find out'). تا introduces the subordinate content/purpose ('so that' / 'in order that' or 'so as to find out'), and کیند is an archaic third-person plural form meaning roughly 'they are' / 'they do' (used here to ask 'who they are'). The sequence 'چه و چون و چند' lists interrogative complements — 'what, how, and how many' — coordinated by و ('and').
segments.s217-g389: همی is an emphatic/archaic particle ('still' / 'even now') placed before the preposition phrase. بر + کدامین ره = 'on which road/way' (کدامین = 'which'), and آید سپاه is 'will the army come' (آید = present/future 'comes/will come', سپاه = 'army'), with the verb preceding the subject here for poetic word order.
segments.s217-g390: که is the relative 'that/which' linking back to سپاه. دارد is the present 'has/possesses' and governs the list of possessions: سراپرده (pavilions/inner tents), و تخت (and a throne), و گاه (and gāh — here poetic/older sense related to place/time/seat). The items are coordinated by و; the clause qualifies which army is meant.
segments.s217-g391: بشد is an archaic past/formative verb here implying 'it happened' or 'they set out' (a classical narrative marker). The personal names بیژن گیو follow as the actors. تا introduces the extent 'as far as / up to' (here 'up to the ridge of the mountain' – تیغ کوه). Word order (verb → names → تا-phrase) reflects epic style inversion.
segments.s217-g392: برآمد is a past verb 'rose/appeared' (here describing the ridge coming into view). بی‌انبوه is an adjectival phrase 'not crowded / sparse', and دور از گروه ('distant from the troop') is a locative phrase with از marking separation. Together they describe the ridge as rising up sparsely and remote from the army/company.
