segments.s217-g145: که introduces a subordinate/relative clause ('that/who'); ایشان is the explicit plural subject 'they'; ز راه دراز is a prepositional phrase 'from the long road' where the adjective دراز follows the noun (typical Persian adjective order in poetry); آمدند is the 3rd-person plural past tense verb 'they came'.
segments.s217-g146: Two coordinated predicates/qualities describing the (same) subject: پراندیشه 'full of thought/pensive' (an adjective/compound adjective) then و + رزمساز 'and war-ready/warrior'; آمدند repeats the 3rd-person plural past verb, with the subject omitted but understood from the previous clause.
segments.s217-g147: ازین آمدن is a prepositional/nominal phrase 'from this coming' (از + این + آمدن) and functions as the topic; بی‌نیازند is the predicate 'they are without need / need not' (3rd-plural); سخت is an adverbial intensifier placed after the verb meaning 'very/utterly' (poetic inversion of normal adverb position). The subject is omitted and understood from prior lines.
segments.s217-g148: خداوند is used in the sense 'lords/sovereigns' here (not the monotheistic God); تاج‌اند is a nominal predicate with the plural copula -اند meaning 'they wear/are crowned' or 'they are the crown/holders of the crown'; و links the next phrase; زیبای تخت reads as a compact adjective + noun 'the beautiful throne' (زیبای functions adjectivally before تخت in this poetic compression).
segments.s217-g149: ندارند is a negated existential/possessive 'they do not have'; سر کم is an idiomatic phrase 'a lesser head' meaning 'inferiority in status' (کم qualifying سر): together this expresses 'they are not inferior'; ز افراسیاب 'than Afrasiab' uses z 'from' in the comparative sense. Subject remains the same (they).
segments.s217-g150: که introduces a clause giving the reason/explanation: با + nouns lists possessions/associations ('with throne and treasure'); the plural copula is attached to the last noun (گنج‌اند) and applies to the whole series, i.e. 'they have/are with throne and treasure'; a second با repeats before 'جاه و آب' to add 'with rank and (social) standing/wealth' — آب here is figurative (prestige/wealth). The conjunctions و coordinate the items.
segments.s217-g151: شوم is a 1st-person aorist/subjunctive 'let me become / let me go / may I be' used with تا to introduce purpose; تا + ببینم 'so that I may see' is a purpose clause (ببینم is 1st-singular subjunctive/present); که here begins an indirect question 'that/to see how...'; چند 'how many' asks quantity and چیند (3rd-plural form of چیدن) asks about arrangement/arraying ('how they arrange/are arrayed').
segments.s217-g152: Two coordinated interrogative phrases: سپهبد کدامند 'which are the spahbeds?' uses کدامند (interrogative 'which are they') with the plural copula -ند; و links the second question; گردان کیند is parallel and asks about the troops/divisions — کیند here is an archaic/poetic 3rd-plural interrogative/capula form ('who/which are they' or 'who composes them'), i.e. 'and who are the units' (subject again understood as those arriving).
segments.s217-g73: ور is a classical conditional particle 'if'. ایدون is a poetic third‑person present/future form 'comes/appears'. کجا is an interrogative/adverb 'where' (here placed after the verb as is common in older Persian). داور آسمان is a genitive phrase 'judge of heaven' (داور = head noun, modified by آسمان).
segments.s217-g74: بشمشیر is an instrumental/adverbial phrase 'with/by the sword'. بر is the preposition 'upon/against'. ما is the object 'us'. سرآرد (from سَرآوردن) is a poetic third‑person present/future 'brings/raises/inflicts'. زمان at the end is the grammatical subject 'time' — classical Persian often places the subject after verb phrases.
segments.s217-g75: ز is the preposition 'from/of'. بخش جهان‌آفرین is a genitive phrase 'the allotment/gift of the World‑Creator' (بخش = portion/allotment, جهان‌آفرین = world‑creator). بیش و کم is a paired expression 'more and less' = 'surplus and shortage'.
segments.s217-g76: نباشد is a negative subjunctive/impersonal clause 'let there not be' / 'there is not'. مپیمای is a negative imperative/jussive form ('م' + پیمای) meaning 'do not measure/judge' (addressing the hearer). بر خیره دم is the measure/standard: بر = 'by/upon', خیره = 'astonished/stunned', دم = 'moment/breath' (here 'moment'). Altogether: 'There is no surplus or lack...; do not judge by a single stunned moment.'
segments.s217-g77: مرا (here split orthographically as م + را) is the experiencer/object 'to me'. مرگ is the noun 'death'. خوشتر is the comparative 'more pleasant / preferable'. بنام بلند is a adjectival phrase 'بنام' = 'called/named' and بلند = 'lofty/high' — literal 'named lofty' (i.e., glorious). The construction places the experiencer first for emphasis: 'To me, death is preferable, called lofty.'
segments.s217-g78: ازین = از + این 'from this' (here 'than this' in the comparative construction خوشتر ... از). زیستن is the gerund 'living'. با introduces accompanying circumstances 'with'. هراس = 'fear' and گزند = 'harm/injury'. The whole phrase completes the comparison: '... than this living with fear and harm.'
segments.s217-g79: برین = بر + این 'on this / about this'. برنهادند is a past tense verb (3rd person plural) 'they raised/uttered' (here 'they uttered'). یکسر is an adverb 'all at once / unanimously', and سخن is the noun 'speech/word' — 'they all spoke at once' or 'they voiced one unanimous speech.'
segments.s217-g80: که is the conjunction 'that' introducing reported content. سالار نیک اختر is a nominal phrase: سالار 'lord/chief', نیک اختر 'good/auspicious‑starred' (an epithet meaning well‑favored by fortune). افگند is a past form 'cast/laid' (here 'laid/founded'), and بن is 'foundation/base'. Together: 'that the noble, well‑starred lord laid the foundation.'
segments.s217-g345: This is a temporal subordinate clause introduced by وزان پس چو — 'وزان پس' = 'from that time on/after that'. چُو here means 'when' (not a simile). روزی is a temporal noun 'a day' or 'the day' and شویم is the 1st-person-plural present-subjunctive/optative 'we shall become/come to' (classical Persian uses the subjunctive to express a future or eventual action). The phrase بایران is an older/poetic spelling of به ایران ('to Iran') where the short vowel and ezāfe-like linking are contracted/omitted in verse.
segments.s217-g346: بنزدیک is an adverb/adverbial phrase 'near, close at hand'. شاه دلیران is a genitive phrase 'the king of the brave' with the ezāfe omitted in poetic registry (دلیران modifies شاه). شویم again is 1st-person-plural subjunctive/optative 'we shall be / we shall come', so the whole segment means 'we shall come near the king of the brave.'
segments.s217-g347: ترا is the object 'you' made of the 2nd-person clitic ت plus the object marker را (ت + را = ترا). پیش تختش is a prepositional/locative phrase 'before/in the presence of his throne' (تختش = تخت + possessive suffix -ش 'his'). برم is 1st-person-singular subjunctive/optative of بردن used like a future 'I will bring/take' and ناگهان is an adverb 'suddenly' (placed after the verb in this line for poetic rhythm).
segments.s217-g348: سرت is 'your head' (سرت = سر + ـت); برفرازم is a poetic verbal/adjectival form meaning 'I exalt/raise (you) high' (built on برفرازیدن; in context it expresses the speaker's act of exalting the addressee). بجاه here means 'in the presence of / before' (literally 'by the dignity/of the presence of'), followed by از مهان 'of/from the guests' — together 'in the presence of the guests.' Word order is verb-object-phrase typical of epic verse and some classical constructions omit connecting particles (ezāfe).
segments.s217-g349: چو here is a simile 'like/as'. باد دمنده is a noun phrase 'the blowing wind' (باد = wind; دمنده = blower/blast). ازان جایگاه = از آن جایگاه 'from that place' (آنان/ازان is an archaic/poetic form meaning 'from that'). The whole segment sets up an image: 'like a gust of wind from that place...'.
segments.s217-g350: برو is the 2nd-person-singular imperative 'go'. سوی is the preposition 'toward'. سالار ایران سپاه is a noun-chain 'the commander of the army of Iran' with the usual epic omission of ezāfe (i.e. سالارِ سپاهِ ایران or سالارِ ایرانِ سپاه). The command addresses a messenger or soldier to proceed toward that commander.
segments.s217-g351: همه هرچ دیدی بدیشان بگوی is an imperative instruction: همه هرچ = 'everything/all that' (همه reinforces هرچ 'whatever'), دیدی is 2nd-person-singular past 'you saw', بدیشان = 'to them' (به + ایشان contracted), and بگوی is a present-subjunctive/imperative form 'say/tell' (classical Persian uses the subjunctive form بگوی as a polite/poetic imperative). Syntax: object phrase 'همه هرچ' + direct object marker/recipient 'بدیشان' + verb 'بگوی'.
segments.s217-g352: سبک باش is an imperative 'be light/brief/tactful' (سبک = light in manner; باش = 2nd-singular imperative of بودن). و is the coordinating 'and'. از هر کسی مژده جوی is a causative/imperative clause: از هر کسی = 'from every/anyone', مژده = 'news/good tidings', and جوی here is the present-subjunctive/imperative of جستن 'seek' (classical poetic form), so 'seek news from everyone.' Note that several verbal imperatives appear in subjunctive shape in classical Persian poetry.
segments.s217-g185: بلکشر is the speaker's name (subject). چنین is an adverb 'thus/so' modifying گفت, which is the past narrative verb 'said' (classical past). هومان is vocative ('O Homan' / the addressee). گرد here is an archaic/poetic verbal or exhortative element (from گردیدن, 'go/turn/about') used with the vocative as a short imperative/exhortation.
segments.s217-g186: که introduces the content clause ('that / (i.e.) ...'). دلرا is دل + the object marker را ('the heart' as direct object). ز کینه is a prepositional phrase 'from/of hatred' giving the quality affecting the heart. نباید is an impersonal modal 'it should not / one must not' and is followed by the infinitival/finite verb سترد meaning 'to remove/conceal/strip' — together 'one should not [do X to] the heart with respect to hatred'.
segments.s217-g187: دو روز is a duration ('for two days') placed before the clause. این یکی functions as the topical subject 'this one / this man'. رنج is the object 'suffering' and بر تن is a prepositional phrase 'upon the body'. نهید is the classical verb form of نهاد/نهادن here meaning 'they placed / they laid' (perfective/past action).
segments.s217-g188: Again دو indicates 'for two (days)'; دیده is 'eye(s)' (here 'two eyes'). بکوه is a directional/adverbial phrase 'toward the mountain' (ب + کوه). هماون ('the same/that' or here a reference to Homaun) specifies the mountain; نهید repeats the verb 'they set/placed' — parallel construction that continues the preceding action for the same duration.
segments.s217-g189: نباید که is the negative impersonal construction 'it should not be that / they must not'. ایشان is the subject pronoun 'they'. شبی here gives the temporal sense '(for) one night' or 'by night' and functions adverbially. بی‌درنگ is an adverb meaning 'without delay / immediately' and modifies the action that follows.
segments.s217-g190: گریزان is a participial adjective 'fleeing' describing the subject; برانند is a classical 3rd‑person plural verbal form (subjunctive/optative flavor here) related to راندن ('to drive/put to flight') used with the sense 'they flee / they depart'. از + این (here written ازین) gives the source 'from this', and جای تنگ is the noun phrase 'narrow/cramped place' serving as the locus they would be leaving.
segments.s217-g191: کنون is an adverb 'now' placed at the start for temporal framing. The series کوه و رود و در و دشت و راه lists locations ('mountain, river, در (here a place-word often 'gate' or 'pass'), plain, and road'); the coordinating و links them in a catalog of terrain.
segments.s217-g192: جهانی is an adjective/adverbial 'worldwide / whole‑world'. شود is the classical 3rd‑person singular form of شدن ('become') used here to express result or emergent state ('will become' / 'becomes'). پردرفش is a compound adjective 'many‑standarded / bannered' describing سپاه, and سپاه is the noun 'army/host' — together 'the bannered army becomes the whole world (i.e., the whole world will be their arena).' Word order places the predicate (جهانی شود) before the noun phrase it comments on, a poetic inversion.
segments.s217-g65: Syntactically this is a verbless/copulative clause typical of poetic Persian: 'همه گرد بر گرد ما' (all around us) is the circumstantial phrase and 'لشکرست' is the predicate, where '-ست' is the archaic/poetic form of 'است' (is). Word order places the adverbial/locative phrase before the predicate for emphasis: literally, 'all around, around us is an army.'
segments.s217-g66: The clause ends with the copula 'خاورست' = 'is (the) east' (poetic contraction of 'خاور است'); the preceding three words form a descriptive noun phrase attributing qualities to 'the east' (i.e. the east is such-and-such). Each noun is juxtaposed as a compact descriptive unit: 'خور' (the sun), 'بارگی' (foliage/leaf), 'خارگر' (hedge- or thorn-worker), so together they characterize the east. This is a compact, image-building genitive-like description rather than an explicit genitive construction.
segments.s217-g67: 'سپه را' uses the older topical/accusative marker 'را' to highlight 'the army' (roughly: 'as for the army'); 'خورش' (cooked food/provisions) is the noun in question. 'بس فراوان نماند' is a negative result statement: 'بس' (enough/very) + 'فراوان' (plentiful) modifies 'نماند' (remained/was left) — here 'نماند' is simple past/perfect sense ‘was not left’ or present with negative force in narrative. Together: 'There remained/was left no abundant food for the army.'
segments.s217-g68: 'جز از' = 'except (from/for)'; the phrase lists the only things left: 'گرز و شمشیر' (mace and sword). 'درمان' is used figuratively as 'remedy' or 'means' (here: the only available 'cure' or recourse), and 'نماند' again states that nothing remains beyond these. Word order is standard older Persian: exception phrase + remainder verb.
segments.s217-g69: بشبگیر is an adverbial/temporal phrase meaning “by night” or “at night.” شمشیرها is the plural noun “swords” with the plural suffix -ها; برکشیم is a 1st‑person plural hortative/subjunctive form of کشیدن here meaning “let us lift/draw (our swords),” with the subject “we” omitted.
segments.s217-g70: همه is an inclusive determiner “all / everyone” modifying the clause. دامن کوه is a genitival phrase “the skirt/foothill of the mountain” (دامن here = foothills), and لشکر is “army/host.” کشیم is the verbal form of کشیدن “to draw/bring/drag,” here 1st‑person plural “let us draw/bring”; the supplied sense list lacked this verbal sense, so it is marked -1.
segments.s217-g71: اگر is the conditional “if.” اختر نیک is a noun + adjective “a good/auspicious star” (i.e. a fortunate star). یاری دهد is a 3rd‑person singular present/subjunctive of دادن with یاری as its object: “grants help.”
segments.s217-g72: بریشان is the fused prepositional phrase بر + ایشان, here “against/to them” (context favors “against them”). مرا را represents the first‑person object “me” with the object marker را (the data segmentation split مرا into م + را); کامگاری is the noun “success/fulfilment,” and دهد is again 3rd‑person singular “gives” — together meaning “(it) gives me success (against them).”
segments.s217-g337: درفشی بدید is a past action 'he saw a banner' (درفشی = 'banner', بدید = past 'saw'). The following nominal group اژدها پیکرش (literally 'dragon — its body') apposes the banner, describing the banner's form: پیکرش ('its body') is a possessive construction that links the image (body) to the banner just mentioned.
segments.s217-g338: پدید آمد is a light-verb combination meaning 'appeared' (پدید + آمد = 'came into view'). The coordinating و links the next nominal phrase; شیر زرین سرش is a verbless nominal clause where 'its head (سرش) (was) a golden lion' is asserted with the copula omitted (typical in poetry).
segments.s217-g339: بدو گفت is 'said to him' (بدو = 'to him', a dative clitic/phrase placed before the verb; گفت here functions as the past verb 'said'). The name or address that follows (انوشه بدی) can be read either as a compound name or as a vocative plus adjective ('Anushé — bad/ugly'); Persian poetry often places the addressee after the verb.
segments.s217-g340: ز دیدار تو دور چشم بدی is a compact predication: ز ('from/because of') + دیدارِ تو ('your sight / seeing you') and the predicate دور چشم بدی literally 'you are distant to the eye' — an idiom meaning 'unpleasant to look at; my eye recoils.' The copula 'است' is omitted as usual in Persian poetry; بدی is the predicative adjective 'are bad/repulsive.'
segments.s217-g341: چو introduces a temporal/conditional clause 'when' (چو = 'when/if/as'); گفتارهای تو آید بجای means 'your words come/are realized in their place' (آید = 'come/occur'; بجای here = 'in place, in due course' or 'take effect'). The phrase expects a continuation (it is setting the condition for what follows).
segments.s217-g342: بدین سان که گفتی is the frozen phrase 'in this way/just as you said' (بدین سان = 'in this manner'; که گفتی = 'that you said'). بپاکیزه رای is an ablative/instrumental phrase 'with pure counsel/opinion' (بپاکیزه = 'pure/chaste', رای = 'opinion/plan'); the initial ب here is the prefix that forms بدین and should be read together with دین as the fixed word 'بدین' rather than as the noun 'دین'.
segments.s217-g343: ببخشمت is a first‑person past/future-idiomatic form meaning 'I grant/give you' (in epic Persian it functions as a promise of gift; the exact tense can be flexible in poetry). چندان گرانمایه چیز is the quantified object 'so many / such precious things' where چندان = 'so much/so many', گرانمایه = 'valuable/precious', and چیز = 'things.'
segments.s217-g344: کزان پس نیازت نیاید بنیز is a result clause: کزان ('from which/so that from it') + پس ('then/thereafter') + نیازت ('your need' with possessive t) + نیاید ('will not come/appear') means 'so that afterwards your need will not arise.' بنیـز (here written as two syllables) is an emphatic particle meaning 'also/anymore' — the whole clause says there will be no further need.
segments.s217-g217: درود جهان‌آفرین بر تو باد is an optative blessing. درود (a noun) is the greeting/blessing being invoked, جهان‌آفرین is a title/appositive naming the source of the blessing, بر تو is a prepositional phrase 'upon you', and باد is the optative particle (classical use of باد = 'may it be'). Word order is noun + apposition + prepositional phrase + optative.
segments.s217-g218: که introduces a subordinate clause giving the reason/result. کردی is the past 2nd-singular form of کردن used here as a main/prefatory verb ('you did'); بپرسش is the verb 'ask' with the enclitic object -ش ('ask it/about it') in a non-imperative form (here part of the reported action). دل بنده شاد is a nominal result clause with an omitted copula: 'the servant's heart (is) glad' (بنده used as humble self-reference).
segments.s217-g219: ببخت تو is a classical/poetic locution meaning 'by/at your fortune' (contrast modern به بختِ تو); شادانم is the adjective شادان with the 1st‑person suffix -م ('I am joyful'). و connects the next nominal phrase; تن درست is a verbless predicate 'the body (is) sound/proper' with the copula omitted — a common poetic structure.
segments.s217-g220: روانم is 'my soul/spirit' with the possessive suffix -م. همی is an adverbial particle meaning 'always/ever' (classical usage). خاک پای تو is a genitive phrase 'the dust of your feet' (خاک = dust, پای تو = your foot), and جست is the simple past 3rd/1st/sg form of جستن here meaning 'leapt/sprang' — together 'my spirit ever leaps to the dust of your feet.' Word order places the adverb and object before the verb in the classical/persian poetic style.
segments.s217-g221: از ایرانیان is a prepositional phrase 'from/among the Iranians' marking the source of the information; هرچ is an indefinite quantifier 'whatever/anything that', پرسید is the past 3rd‑person singular 'asked', and شاه is the subject 'the king' placed after the verb (a common poetic inversion: V + S).
segments.s217-g222: نه ... و نه is the correlative negative pair 'neither ... nor'. Each noun (گنج, سپاه, تاج, گاه) is presented without an explicit copula; سپاهست is an archaic contraction of سپاه است ('(there is) an army' or 'troops') where the copula is attached. گاه here means 'place/time/position' (poetic for 'throne/seat'). The whole means there was no treasure, no troops, nor crown nor seat.
segments.s217-g223: بی‌اندازه is an adverb/adjective 'immeasurably/excessively' modifying پیکار; پیکار is 'battle/fighting'. جستند is the past plural of جستن used in the idiomatic sense 'they engaged in / they sought' (here 'they waged/fought'). The subject is omitted (understood 'they') as typical in Persian.
segments.s217-g224: ندارند is the present 3rd‑person plural 'they do not have/possess'. از جنگ جز introduces the excluded remainder 'from war (they have) except', where جز means 'except' and sets up the sole thing they possess. خاره سنگ is a compound meaning small stones/pebbles (خاره = pebble, سنگ = stone). The clause means they have nothing from warfare except pebbles and stones.
segments.s217-g289: سوی باختر is an adverbial phrase meaning 'toward the west' (سوی = toward, باختر = west). گشت is the past-tense intransitive verb 'turned/became' (3sg). گیتی (the subject 'the world') and the reason phrase ز گرد ('from/about all around') follow due to poetic inversion; ز is the poetic preposition 'from' and گرد means 'around'.
segments.s217-g290: سراسر is an adverb 'throughout' or 'all over'; بسان introduces a simile 'like/as'; شب لاژورد is a noun phrase 'a lapis‑blue night' where لاژورد qualifies شب (color/metaphor). There is no copula — a verbless comparative image: 'all over like a lapis‑blue night.'
segments.s217-g291: شد is the past verb 'became' (3sg). از خاک is an ablative phrase 'out of/from the dust/earth' giving the cause or source. خورشید تابان is the subject noun phrase 'the radiant/shining sun' that follows the verb by poetic word order. بنفش is the predicate adjective 'purple' describing the sun after شد.
segments.s217-g292: ز here functions as a causal 'because of' (ز بس = 'because of so many'); بس expresses abundance ('so many/so much'). The clause بر پشت پیلان درفش is a prepositional phrase 'on the backs of the elephants [a] banner' with بر = 'on/upon', پشت = 'back', پیلان = plural 'elephants' and درفش 'banner'. Note the verb/copula is omitted (a common poetic device): the banner is presented as being on the elephants' backs.
segments.s217-g293: غو is a noun 'uproar/tumult'. دیده here is 'with (his) eye' (instrumental sense), and بشنید is the past verb 'heard' (3sg); together the phrase reports that Goudarz perceived the tumult. گودرز is the subject (proper name) and و گفت links to his ensuing speech — گفت is the past tense 'he said' (poetic diction, though the sense-listing given is nominal).
segments.s217-g294: که introduces reported content ('that'); جز is the exception word 'except'; خاک تیره is a noun phrase 'dark earth' (a metonym for the grave). نداریم is present-negative 'we do not have/possess' (1pl), and جفت here means 'companion/partner' (i.e. 'we have no companion but the dark earth' — a grim formula meaning only burial awaits).
segments.s217-g295: رخش is the steed (proper name/Rokhsh) as subject. گشت is the past intransitive 'became/turned'. ز اندوه gives the cause 'from sorrow'. برسان قیر is a compressed comparative: برسان here links to قیر ('to/into pitch'), producing the image 'became as black as pitch' — قیر functions as the comparative referent.
segments.s217-g296: چنان شد is a formulaic 'it became so' or 'thus it happened'. کجا normally means 'where' and introduces the following clause; خسته is 'tired/weary' and گردد is a subjunctive/present form 'become' (here expressing resulting or potential state). بتیر is an ablative/agent phrase 'by an arrow' or 'from an arrow'. The clause is elliptical and poetic: 'it became such that (one) would be made weary/by an arrow' — the imagery is compressed and the exact syntactic subject is left implicit.
segments.s217-g265: نباشد is a negative optative/subjunctive (third person) used like a hortative: “let there not be.” ز is the poetic preposition “from/of.” یزدان is the divine agent “God.” کسی here has the sense ‘no one’ (negated by the optative). ناامید is the predicative adjective “hopeless/despairing,” completing the clause.
segments.s217-g266: وگر is a concessive/conditional conjunction “and if/even if.” شب شود uses شود (subjunctive/optative or future‑oriented present) meaning “should become / fall (night).” روی روز is a genitive phrase “the face of the day” (the ezāfe is omitted in poetic diction); سپید is a post‑nominal adjective “white/bright,” functioning predicatively (‘the day’s face is bright’).
segments.s217-g267: بیک روز is a poetic temporal phrase meaning “on a certain/one day” (بیک is archaic/poetic); کز is the relative “which/that/from which.” ما is the first‑person plural “we/us.” نجستند is the past plural of جستن, here “sought/attempted” (they sought); جنگ is the object “war/battle.” Word order is somewhat inverted as is common in poetry.
segments.s217-g268: مکن is the negative imperative “do not.” دل is the affected noun “heart.” ز اندیشه marks cause/source (“out of/from thought” = “because of thought”). بر is a directional/prepositional particle here meaning roughly “into/on.” خیره تنگ is a compound adjectival description: خیره “astonished/stunned” + تنگ “narrow/tight,” describing the state the heart should not be brought into.
segments.s217-g269: نبستند is a past negative 3rd‑person plural of بستن used here in the idiomatic sense “they did not ordain/set/inscribe.” بر ما در آسمان is a locative/explanatory phrase “for us / upon us in the sky/heavens” (i.e. in the heavenly decree/fate).
segments.s217-g270: بپایان رسد is a light/verbal phrase meaning “come to an end” (بـ + پایان + رسد), with رسد in the 3rd‑person form “reach/attain/happen.” هر بد بدگمان is a nominal group “every bad/evil suspicious thought” (هر = every; بد = bad; بدگمان = suspicious/dubious).
segments.s217-g271: اگر is the conditional particle 'if'. بخشش (bakhshesh) is a noun 'forgiveness' and stands here in a genitive relationship with کردگار (kardegār) — the ezāfe linking vowel is written only implicitly in classical/persicized spelling. کردگار بلند reads literally 'the Lofty Creator' (کردگار = God, بلند = 'lofty/exalted' postposed as an epithet). Together the phrase means 'if the Forgiveness of the Lofty Creator...'.
segments.s217-g272: چنانست is the fused classical form of چنان است 'is such'. کاید here should be read as a contraction of که آید ('ke āyad' = 'that comes / may come'), so it introduces the result clause. بمابر is written together but parses as به ما بر ('be mā bar') — i.e. 'upon us' (with بر indicating 'fall/upon' in the sense 'befall'). گزند is the noun 'harm' that is the thing which 'comes upon us'. The whole segment: 'is such that harm may befall us.'
segments.s217-g113: بگفتند is a 3rd‑person plural past-tense/perfective verbal phrase ('they said'). کای is a vocative particle (older form of ای) addressing someone: 'O ...'. نامور پهلوان is a noun phrase 'renowned champion/hero' following the vocative; word order is verb → vocative → noun phrase because the speech report opens with the verb.
segments.s217-g114: همیشه is an adverb 'always'. بزی is an archaic/poetic optative/imperative from زیستن ('may you live' / 'live!') expressing a wish directed to the addressee. شاد و روشن‌روان are predicative adjectives ('happy and bright‑souled/clear‑minded') describing the wished state; the clause is therefore an optative blessing: 'may you always live happy and bright‑soul'd.'
segments.s217-g115: بدیدار شاهان is a prepositional/temporal phrase literal 'at (the) meeting/sight of kings' (بدیدار = 'on seeing/meeting'). دلت is دل + 2nd‑person suffix (your heart). شاددار is a hortative/imperative construction (شاد + دار, 'keep/be joyful' or 'let be joyful'); the phrase as a whole is a wish: 'at the meeting of kings, may your heart be joyful.'
segments.s217-g116: روانت is روان + 2nd‑person suffix ('your soul/spirit'). ز is the poetic preposition 'from' (here 'free from' = 'free of, without'). اندیشه is the noun 'thought/worry'. آزاد دار is a compound verbal instruction (آزاد + دار = 'keep/hold free' or 'make/keep free'); together the clause means 'keep your spirit free from thought/worry.'
segments.s217-g117: ز ... تا frames a range/extent: ز کشمیر تا ... = 'from Kashmir to ...'. تا here means 'as far as / even to'. برتر از is a comparative 'higher/beyond/superior to'. رود شهد is a genitive phrase 'river of honey' (poetic word order; شهد = 'honey' modifying رود). Together: 'from Kashmir up to beyond the river of honey.'
segments.s217-g118: This is a nominal list: درفش 'banner/standard', و conjunction 'and', سپاهست is a poetic fused form meaning 'a host/army (is)' (سپاه + -ست/copy of 'is' or idiomatic nominal), و پیلان 'and elephants' (پیلان = plural of پیل), و مهد 'and cradle/birthplace'. The copula is compressed in poetic diction (سپاهست supplies existence/being).
segments.s217-g119: نخست is an adverb 'first, at first'. اندر آیم is 'I come/enter' (آیم is 1st‑person present/subjunctive used narratively as past/future depending on context). ز marks source 'from' (poetic use). خاقان چین = 'the Khāqān (emperor) of China' (چین = China). The clause reads 'first I came from the Khāqān of China' (i.e. I had been at/come from his court).
segments.s217-g120: که introduces a relative clause 'whose/that'. تاجش = 'his crown' (تاج + possessive -ش). سپهرست is a poetic predicate: سپهر (sky/heaven) + -ست ('is') ⇒ 'his crown is the sky/heaven.' و تختش زمین continues in parallel; تختش = 'his throne' and زمین = 'earth', with the copula understood/omitted in the second conjunct (elliptical): 'and his throne (is) the earth.'
segments.s217-g225: چو here is the subordinating conjunction 'when' introducing a temporal clause. The three adjectives بی‌کام، بی‌نام، بی‌تن are coordinated by و and describe the subject; بی‑+noun is an older/poetic negating prefix meaning 'without' or 'deprived of'. شدند is the past plural verb 'they became / they were', placed at the end as the finite predicate.
segments.s217-g226: گریزان is a present participle/adjectival predicate ('fleeing') that describes the subject's state; بکوه is the contracted prepositional phrase به کوه 'to the mountain' (movement goal). هماون is a demonstrative specifying which mountain ('that same'), modifying کوه; شدند is the past plural copular/auxiliary 'they became / they were' completing the clause and agreeing with the plural subject.
segments.s217-g227: سپهدار طوس is a noun phrase with title + name (title first, name after). است is the copula 'is' linking the subject to the predicate. مردی دلیر is the predicate nominal phrase: مردی is indefinite 'a man' (Persian has no article, so مردی indicates 'a certain man' or 'a man') and دلیر is the adjective 'brave' that modifies it.
segments.s217-g228: بهامون is an archaic/poetic compound meaning 'for us' / 'on our behalf' (به + ما with an old ending). نترسد is the present-tense negative form 'he does not fear' (ن- prefix + present stem), used here as a general/habitual assertion about the subject. ز is the poetic preposition 'from / because of' introducing the cause or object of fear; پیکار شیر is a genitive-like grouping 'battle (with) the lion' where شیر ('lion') specifies the kind of battle.
segments.s217-g229: بزرگان is the plural subject 'the great ones / elders'. چو here means 'like' (a comparative marker) and introduces an example: چو گودرز places the name گودرز in apposition to بزرگان. کشوادگان is an epithet/title attached to گودرز (in apposition) and follows the name, a common classical/epic ordering.
segments.s217-g230: The parallel structure repeats چو ('like') to list examples: چو گیو و چو رهام 'like Giv and like Roham'. ز آزادگان is a poetic prepositional phrase meaning 'of/from the freemen' — here indicating social group or membership ('among the nobly free'). The whole segment provides further instances among the بزرگان.
segments.s217-g231: This is a noun phrase made of stacked epithets before the title: ببخت 'ill‑fated' and سرافراز 'lofty/triumphant' both modify خاقان 'khaghan' (ruler). چین follows خاقان as a place-name complement 'of China' (title + domain). Placing epithets before the noun is a poetic unit emphasizing these qualities.
segments.s217-g232: سپهبد is the subject 'the spahbed / general'. نبیند is the negative present 'does not see' or 'will not see' used here in a generalizing narrative sense. سپه را uses را as the definite object marker attached to سپه ('the army'), and جزین is the restrictive expression 'except this' (جز + demonstrative), placed at the end to limit the scope: 'sees no army except this one.'
segments.s217-g201: This clause introduces an alternative/indirect question-like idea: 'whether this is X or Y'. که opens the clause ('that/whether'); تا here helps frame the alternatives ('whether / even so'); این is the demonstrative 'this'; بهشتست is a nominal predicate 'is heaven/paradise' (بهشت + copular -ست). یا links the two alternatives and رزمگاه is the second nominal option 'battlefield'. Word order is Subject-predicate (this — is heaven) and the whole functions as the first half of a balanced antithesis.
segments.s217-g202: This clause gives the comment that follows the alternative: سپهر 'the sky/heavens' is the topic; برینست‌گر is a conditional/ concessive compound meaning roughly 'if it is superior / if so' (the older -گر following an adjective). تاج و گاه ('crown and place/occasion') are the things being compared to the heavens. In other words, the heavens are declared loftier than crown and throne; the clause is elliptical (the copula/adverbial relation is understood).
segments.s217-g203: بیامد is a past-tense verb 'came'. بنزدیک is an adverbial phrase 'near / up close' modifying the verb. خاقان is the subject 'khagan' (a title). چین here is an appositive/noun 'China' identifying that khagan as the Chinese ruler (i.e. 'the khagan of China'). The word order is verb first (poetic inversion) followed by location/adverb then the title and its specifier.
segments.s217-g204: پیاده functions adverbially here 'on foot' (or 'having dismounted' in context) and modifies the following verb. ببوسید is past 'kissed' (he kissed). روی زمین is a prepositional phrase 'on the ground' where روی is the preposition 'upon/on' and زمین is 'earth/ground'. The sequence describes the khagan approaching on foot and kissing the ground.
segments.s217-g205: چو is a temporal subordinator 'when'. خاقان is subject 'the khagan'. بدیدش is the past verb 'saw him' (بدید + object clitic -ش). به بر in this construction marks motion toward or into the embrace ('to/into (his) bosom'); درگرفت is a past-stem verb here meaning 'seized / took in his arms / embraced' (an idiomatic use in classical Persian). The clause describes a quick action immediately following the khagan's seeing him.
segments.s217-g206: بماند is past 'remained / stood' but here idiomatically 'was left / stood (in a state)'. از introduces the cause/trigger of astonishment ('at / because of'). بر and و link the two items that astonished the onlookers: یال پیران (literally 'the mane/crest of the elders' — i.e. their splendor or venerable appearance). شگفت is the predicate/adjective 'astonished / wondrous', describing the reaction. Overall: 'they stood amazed at his robe/appearance and the elders' glory.'
segments.s217-g207: بپرسید is past 'he asked' (questioned). بسیار here modifies the verb and means 'much / at length / many [questions]' — i.e. he questioned him extensively. و simply links the next action. بنواختش is an older form of نواختن with object clitic -ش; in poetic context it can mean 'took hold of / handled / struck' and here functions as a transitive action toward the visitor (contextually 'took him up' or 'received him').
segments.s217-g208: بر خویش is a reflexive/directional phrase 'to himself' (بر + خوی + pronominal ش = 'upon his own/self'); note that classical Persian often splits خویش into خوی + ش. نزدیک is an adverb 'near / close'. بنشاختش is the past causative/putting verb 'he set/placed him' (ب + نشاختن), here 'he seated him' or 'set him down' near himself. The phrase describes bringing the guest close and seating him.
segments.s217-g97: 'ز' is the poetic preposition 'from' placed before its noun phrase; 'مرز سپیجاب' = 'the border/frontier of Sepijab'. 'تا' links that place to the endpoint 'دشت روم' and here means 'as far as' (indicating extent). Word order is preposition + noun, then 'تا' + target-place.
segments.s217-g98: 'سپاهی' is a noun referring to a host/army (collective). The relative clause 'که بود' uses past-tense 'بود' ('was') to qualify that army; 'اندر' = 'in/within' and 'آباد بوم' = 'prosperous/inhabited land' (adjective + noun). The whole segment states where that army was located.
segments.s217-g99: 'فرستادم' is 1st-person past ('I sent/I dispatched'); the subject is omitted (typical Persian). 'اینک' is an adverb 'now' inserted for emphasis. 'سوی کارزار' is a prepositional phrase 'toward the battlefield' with 'سوی' = 'toward(s)'.
segments.s217-g100: 'برآرند' is a 3rd-person plural verbal form used optatively/subjunctively ('that they may bring/inflict'). Here it expresses purpose/result connected to the previous 'I sent' (i.e. 'I sent (them) so that they might bring ...'). 'از' introduces the victims/target of the calamity in the idiom 'دِمار از کسی درآوردن' = 'to wreak havoc on someone', so 'از طوس و خسرو دمار' = 'devastation upon Tous and Khosrow'.
segments.s217-g101: 'چو' = 'when' introduces a temporal clause. 'بشنید' is past ('heard'); the agent 'پیران' (the elders) follows the verb here because of poetic inversion. 'بتوران سپاه' indicates the army's movement or presence 'into/at Turan' (بتوران = 'to/into Turan'), so the phrase means 'when the elders heard that the army had gone into Turan.'
segments.s217-g102: 'چنین گفت' = 'thus (he) said' (past). What follows is a vocative phrase introduced by 'کای' (= 'O'); 'سرفرازان' is the vocative plural 'you proud/victorious ones'. 'شاه' names the speaker (the king) and, in this poetic order, appears with the reported speech. Overall: 'Thus the king said: "O you proud ones..."'.
segments.s217-g103: 'بدین مژده' = 'with this tidings' or 'on this good news' where 'بدین' is a fused prepositional form ('به' + 'این') meaning 'with/by this'. 'شاه پیر و جوان' addresses the king and (by extension) both elders and youths — poetic apposition: 'O king, old and young'.
segments.s217-g104: 'همه' = 'all/everyone'; 'شاد باشید' is an imperative/subjunctive 2nd-person plural ('be joyful'); 'و روشن‌روان' is an adjectival epithet 'and bright‑souled/cheerful', functioning as a complement/wish. The line is an address: 'Be all joyful and of merry/bright spirits.'
segments.s217-g401: اگر introduces a conditional clause. بشمری is the present/subjunctive/imperfective stem of شمردن ('to count'/'to reckon') used impersonally or as 'if one counts/if you count'; نیست negates the existence of that counting/measure: 'there is no counting/measure.' انداز و مر is a poetic phrasing where و stands in place of the ezāfe and مر is an older/clitic form meaning 'me'/'my' — together 'انداز و مر' = 'my measure/estimate.'
segments.s217-g402: همی is an emphatic/continuative particle placed before the verb. از تبیره شود is a light-clause meaning 'becomes/turns (so) from explanation' with شود as the 3rd-singular past/future-of-become; گوش کر is a noun + adjective ('ear deaf') functioning as the resulting state — literally 'the ear becomes deaf (to explanation).' Word order (adverb + prepositional phrase + verb + predicate) is typical classical Persian.
segments.s217-g403: سپهبد is the subject (a title). چو is the temporal conjunction 'when,' and بشنید is the 3rd-person past of شنیدن ('he heard'). گفتار اوی is the object phrase 'his speech/words' with اوی as the enclitic possessive 'his.' The clause order is Subject — (when) Verb — Object, as normal in older Persian narration.
segments.s217-g404: دلش is 'his heart' with the possessive clitic ش attached. گشت is the past of گشتن used as 'became/turned (into).' پر is an adjective 'full (of)' that precedes the noun it modifies (درد). The construction 'دلش گشت پر درد' = 'his heart became full of grief.' The second conjunct 'و پر آب روی' continues the predicate: 'and (his) face became full of water' (i.e., tears); روی follows the descriptive phrase 'پر آب.'
segments.s217-g405: سران سپه را همه is the definite object group ('all the chiefs/heads of the army') with را marking the direct object and همه emphasizing totality. گرد کرد is a compound verbal idea ('to round up' / 'to gather together'), literally 'made (them) round/about' — a common classical way to say 'he gathered (them).' The subject is omitted (understood from previous lines).
segments.s217-g406: This is a nominal/result clause describing the spahbed's emotional reaction: بسی here functions as an intensifier 'very/much,' گرم means 'ardent/zealous,' و links the two qualities, and تیمار لشکر is 'care/solicitude for the army.' بخورد is used impersonally/predicatively ('befell' or 'came upon him') — i.e., 'great zeal and care for the army came upon him' or 'he felt great zeal and took great care of the troops.'
segments.s217-g407: چنین گفت is 'thus he said' (چنین = 'thus/so', گفت = past 'said' — note the provided lemma senses list does not include the verbal past meaning). کز is a fused form of 'که + ز' and means 'that from' / 'because of,' introducing the reason clause. گردش روزگار is a genitive phrase 'the turning/changes of fate' (گردش + روزگار = vicissitudes of time/fate).
segments.s217-g408: نبینم is the negative present (or gnomic present) 'I do not see' or 'I see not.' همی is an emphatic/continuative particle adding force ('indeed/at all'), and جز meaning 'except' introduces the only thing perceived: غم کارزار 'the grief/sorrow of battle' (کارزار = battle/struggle). The clause reads 'I see nothing, indeed, except the sorrow of war.'
segments.s217-g137: This is a verbless nominal clause: the pronoun 'تو' is the subject and the copula 'هستی' is omitted (typical in epic Persian). The coordinator 'و' simply links the two adjectives.
segments.s217-g138: برزبان کهنی/شاعری: 'کزین' = 'از این' ('from/through this'); the clause has passive or intransitive 'شود' (become) with the subject 'مرد پیر' ('the old man') placed after the verb (Poetic word order). Here 'بر' + 'نا' together form the adjective 'برنا' ('youthful'), so 'بر + نا + شود' = 'becomes young' — the individual parts 'بر' and 'نا' are functioning as a morphologically bound unit rather than their standalone senses.
segments.s217-g139: 'ز' (poetic variant of 'از') marks position/association here: 'with/in the army of yours'. 'توی' is colloquial/poetic 'in, among'. 'پهلو و پیش‌رو' are two locative nouns meaning 'flank and front' describing positions within the army; the whole phrase is a locative prepositional group placed before the predicate.
segments.s217-g140: 'همیشه' (always) modifies the optative/predicative clause that follows. 'بزی' is an archaic/poetic optative/second‑person form meaning 'may you live' (not the literal 'goat'); the copula 'باشی' is contracted/archaic here. 'شاد' is the adjectival complement 'happy/joyful'. The clause then adds 'و فرمانت نو' ('and your command [be] new') where the copula is again omitted and 'فرمانت' is the possessed noun (command + clitic -ت) modified by 'نو' (renewed/new).
segments.s217-g141: A compound subject 'دل و جان' ('heart and life') with the genitive 'پیران' ('of the elders') after it — poetic word order putting possessed phrase before possessor. 'پر از خنده' = 'full of laughter' where 'پر' = 'full (of)' governs the 'از' + noun complement. 'گشت' is the past intransitive 'became/was made' (گشت = became).
segments.s217-g142: 'تو گفتی' is the simple past 'you said' (second person). The particle 'مگر' here functions rhetorically ('is it not that...?/could it be that...?') introducing the surprised/questioning content. The remainder 'مرده ... زنده گشت' states that the dead have become alive; 'گشت' again is 'became'.
segments.s217-g143: 'بهومان' here functions as the addressee/vocative (likely a proper name or title) and begins the clause. 'چنین گفت پیران' literally 'thus said the elders' — word order places the indirect object/address before the verb and the agent 'پیران' after it. 'که' introduces the content clause and 'من' is the subject of that clause.
segments.s217-g144: 'پذیره' (reception/acceptance) is used as the object/goal and combines with the optative/subjunctive verb 'شوم' ('may I be' / 'that I become') to express desire/request. 'پیش این انجمن' is a locative phrase 'before/in the presence of this assembly' that completes the clause. The subjunctive 'شوم' marks reported desire or polite wish.
segments.s217-g105: بباید is a modal auxiliary meaning 'must/ought' (poetic duplicated form instead of ساده 'باید'); کنون is a temporal adverb 'now'; دل is the direct object 'heart'; ز + تیمار is a prepositional phrase 'from/of care/worry'; شست is the perfective/past form 'washed' placed at the end (verb-final order) so the whole clause reads 'now (I) must wash the heart from care' — a poetic way to say 'I must free my heart of worry'.
segments.s217-g106: This clause continues with a negated intransitive verb phrase: بایران is a poetic/ambiguous place-form (here functioning like a locative), نمانم is the 1st-person negative present/subjunctive 'I will not remain/stay' (‑م marks 1sg), بر و بوم is a binomial 'hearth and homeland/native soil' (بر used as part of the idiom, literally 'on/ upon' + بوم), and رست is a short verb used poetically (here 'to be delivered/escapе/to be at rest'); overall the sequence expresses 'I will not remain in (that) land nor on hearth and home and will not be at rest/shall depart.'
segments.s217-g107: سر here (literally 'head') is used idiomatically with the construction 'سر از ... خواستن' to indicate being freed or rid of something; از رزم و از رنج و کین lists three things 'from battle, from suffering, and (from) hatred/enmity'; خواستن is the infinitive 'to want' (used here to express the desire/intention to be rid of those things). Word order is typical Persian listing of objects followed by the verbal infinitive.
segments.s217-g108: برآسود is an adjectival/predicative past form 'was/ became at rest' or 'found repose'; وز is an archaic/poetic preposition here meaning 'from/of' (its ordinary noun senses do not apply — marked below if senses don't match); لشکر is the object 'army/host'; آراستن is the infinitive 'to array/prepare/equip' — syntactically this part pairs two infinitive-like ideas ('to find rest' and 'to array the army') expressing desired outcomes or results.
segments.s217-g109: This is a locational list: بایران (poetic locative for 'in Iran' or 'in those lands' — ambiguous in the supplied senses), و links, توران is the place-name 'Turan', و separates, بر + خشک و آب means 'upon/on dry land and water' (بر is the preposition 'on/upon' and خشک 'dry/arid' contrasts with آب 'water'). The sequence enumerates spheres ('Iran and Turan, on dry land and on water').
segments.s217-g110: نبینند is the 3rd‑person plural present/subjunctive or optative form 'they see' (in negative contexts often translated 'they see nothing'); جز is the preposition 'except'; کام is the noun 'desire/wish'; افراسیاب is the proper name 'Afrasiab' as the possessor/subject of that desire. The phrase says they see nothing except the desire of Afrasiab.
segments.s217-g111: ز is the preposition 'from'; لشکر is 'the army/host' as the source; بر here functions as 'toward/to' introducing the target; پهلوان is 'the champion/hero' (the recipient of the action); پیش رو is a compound meaning 'in front/foremost' (پیش 'before/in front', رو 'toward/face') — the whole phrase means 'from the army to the champion at the front'.
segments.s217-g112: بمژده is an adverbial/participial form 'with good tidings' (marks the manner); بیامد is the simple past 'came' (3rd person); همی is a frequentive/intensifying particle (poetic, here 'indeed/just' or continuing sense); نو is the adjective 'new'; بنو is a noun (here probably a proper/clan-name or title 'Banu') — together 'with good tidings came a new Banu' (the exact referent of بنو is poetic/idiomatic and contextually an arriving person or group).
segments.s217-g161: هرانکس که هستند is a relative clause 'whoever (هر آنکس) who are (که هستند)'. زیشان is an archaic/prepositional form meaning 'of/among them' that attaches before the head noun. سران is the plural head noun 'leaders/heads' (object of what follows). Word order is typical poetic Persian: relative phrase + prepositional marker + head noun.
segments.s217-g162: کنم is 1st-person present/subjunctive used here as a future/promise 'I will (do)'. پای و گردن are direct objects 'feet and neck' that follow the verb in this poetic order; ببندگران is an archaic/poetic formation related to 'bind' (agentive/verb-form) so the whole cluster expresses 'I will bind their feet and necks.' The verb is causative/performative (I will cause/make it so).
segments.s217-g163: فرستم is 1st-person present/subjunctive used as a simple future 'I will send / I send'. بنزدیک افراسیاب is a prepositional/adverbial phrase 'to the vicinity of Afrasiab' (بنزدیک = near/of the vicinity); the verb precedes its locative complement in typical poetic order.
segments.s217-g164: The clause uses the correlative negation نه ... نه ... 'neither ... nor ...'. آرام جویم literally 'I seek calm/rest' (جویم is 1st-person 'I seek'). بدین بر is a compact, somewhat archaic prepositional phrase modifying جویم ('by/for this (expedition/affair)'); here the preposition ب- is attached to دین/دین‑بر in the text as a set phrase. The final خواب is the second item in the neither–nor pair ('nor sleep'). Grammatically, جویم governs آرام and خواب, with بدین بر specifying the domain/condition.
segments.s217-g165: ز لشکر is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from the army/host'. هر آنکس که آید is a free relative 'whichever person who comes' and آید is used generically/potentially ('comes' / 'falls'). بدست here is an adverbial 'into (my) hand' (i.e., falls into my hands), placed after the relative clause in typical Persian order.
segments.s217-g166: سرانشان is the direct object 'their leaders' (the possessive -شان). ببرم is 1st-person present/subjunctive used as a future 'I will take/cut off' (here 'take' / 'seize' in a violent sense). بشمشیر is instrumental ('with the sword' — the prefix ب- marks instrument), and پست is an adjectival/pejorative qualifier 'base/despicable' describing how they will be treated or the character of those leaders.
segments.s217-g167: بسوزم here is a 1st-person subjunctive/present used as a futureive 'I will burn' (a hostile resolution). خاک ایشان 'their soil/land' is the object (possessive ایشان 'their'), and بباد is an idiom 'to/into the wind' meaning 'drive away/disperse' — together: 'I will burn and cast their land to the wind' (i.e., destroy it).
segments.s217-g168: نگیریم is 1st-person plural subjunctive/negative 'we will not take / we shall not seize' (used here as a firm decision about the aftermath). زان = 'from that / of that', and بوم و بر is a fixed phrase 'homeland and district/region'. نیز یاد functions together as 'not even a remembrance/trace' (نیز intensifies the negation and یاد is the small remnant/trace). The line says they will take nothing at all from that land — not even a memory.
segments.s217-g321: شوم is an optative/subjunctive of شدن here ('let me be / let me'), followed by the present-subjunctive compound پر کن(م) 'let me fill'. چشم و آغوش را is the coordinated definite object (the object marker را is attached only to آغوش but scopes over both coordinated nouns) — literally 'fill the eyes and the embrace/bosom.' Word order reflects a poetic sequence: optative + verb phrase + object.
segments.s217-g322: بگیرم is first‑person subjunctive/jussive of گرفتن ('let me seize/take'). The following ببر functions as a second verb in sequence meaning 'carry/take away' (here supplied as an imperative/jussive form in poetry). گیو و شیدوش را is the coordinated, definite direct object (را marks both names); the verbs form a compound action 'seize and carry off Giv and Shidūsh.'
segments.s217-g323: همان is a demonstrative meaning 'that same' or 'the very,' immediately specifying the persons named. The sequence lists proper names (بیژن، گیو، رهام) followed by the object marker را, making them definite/targeted individuals in the utterance.
segments.s217-g324: سواران جنگی is a noun + adjective phrase 'warlike horsemen / military riders.' و خودکام را adds 'and the autocratic/tyrannical (ones)' as a coordinated noun phrase; the final را again marks the coordinated phrase as definite/object. Adjectives follow the noun in Persian (سواران جنگی = 'horsemen who are warlike').
segments.s217-g325: به + noun + کردن (به پدرود کردن) forms a circumstantial/gerundial expression 'in/by bidding farewell.' رخ هر کسی is 'the face of each person' (هر = 'each/every', کسی = 'person/one'). The structure places the circumstance ('in bidding farewell') before the action that follows.
segments.s217-g326: ببوسم is first‑person subjunctive/jussive of بوسیدن ('let me kiss' / 'I will kiss') and pairs with the previous gerundial phrase to mean 'in bidding farewell I will kiss...'. ببارم is a verb literally 'let fall / rain down' used figuratively for shedding tears; ز مژگان بسی is a prepositional phrase 'from (my) eyelashes, much' (ز = 'from', مژگان = 'eyelashes', بسی = 'much/enough').
segments.s217-g327: نهادند is the simple past, third‑person plural of نهادن 'they placed/put.' زین بر سمند is a prepositional phrase 'the saddle upon the steed' (زین = saddle, بر = on/upon, سمند = steed). چمان is a demonstrative/particle meaning 'so/thus' or 'in this manner,' modifying the placing action.
segments.s217-g328: خروش آمد is a simple verbal phrase 'a cry/roar came' (خروش = cry/roar, آمد = came). از دیده هم indicates the source ('from the eye/eyes') and هم adds 'also' or 'too'; در زمان places it temporally ('at that time / in that moment'). Together they report a cry issuing from the eye at the same moment.
segments.s217-g313: درفش سپهدار ایران is a genitive chain: درفش (head noun) + سپهدار ایران (the commander of Iran) — i.e. 'the banner of the commander of Iran'. کجاست is the fused question-word + copula (کجا + است), here spelled poetically ست; it means 'where is it?'.
segments.s217-g314: نگه کن is an imperative (نگه = 'watch/guard' + کن = 'do' → 'keep watch'). چپ لشکر is a substantivized/adjectival phrase 'the left (flank) of the army' (چپ used as noun), and دست راست coordinates with it ('and the right hand/flank'). The object marker is omitted as is typical in imperatives; راست here indicates the right side (direction).
segments.s217-g315: بدو here is the prepositional/Pronominal 'to him' (not the imperative 'run'). دیده‌بان is the subject 'the sentinel', گفت functions as 'said', and کز is the archaic subordinator 'that/from which' introducing the subordinate clause; هر دو روی means 'both sides' (روی = 'side/surface' here).
segments.s217-g316: نه before the verb negates it ('I do not see'). بینم is first-person present of 'see' (here with the archaic particle همی, which gives a continuous/emphatic sense). جنبش و گفت‌وگوی are the coordinated objects: 'movement and conversation/talking'. The word order (negation + verb + objects) is normal for older/poetic Persian.
segments.s217-g317: ازان کار ('from that affair') is an adverbial phrase giving cause. شد is the past auxiliary/verb 'became/was', followed by the subject پهلوان (inverted order typical of poetry). پر ز درد is a predicate phrase meaning 'full of pain' (پر before ز is a poetic order for modern پر از).
segments.s217-g318: فرود ریخت is a compound verbal idea 'poured/fell down' (فرود 'down/descending' + ریخت 'poured/flowed'). از دیدگان marks the source 'from (his) eyes', and آب زرد is the object 'yellow water' (a poetic expression for tears). Word order places the verbal phrase before source and object in the poetic line.
segments.s217-g319: بنالید is a past-tense verb 'he moaned/lamented'; و گفت links to speech that follows. اسپ را زین کنید is an imperative addressed to attendants: اسپ ('steed/horse') + را (definite object marker) + زین ('saddle') + کنید (plural imperative 'do/make') → 'Saddle the horse.'
segments.s217-g320: ازین پس is an adverbial phrase 'from now on' (از + این/این‌ 'this' + پس 'after/then'). مرا (here split orthographically as م + را in the provided segmentation) is the direct object 'me'. خشت بالین کنید is an imperative clause 'make (for) my pillow a brick' (خشت = mudbrick, بالین = pillow/bedside); together it means 'henceforth lay a brick for my pillow' — a request to prepare a simple burial/bed.
segments.s217-g57: سوی is a preposition meaning 'toward' and precedes its noun (خیمه = 'tent'). رفتند is past plural 'they went'. زان (ز + آن) = 'from that' introduces a link to the following noun رزمگاه ('battlefield'). Word order is preposition + place, verb, then the adverbial qualifier 'from that battlefield'.
segments.s217-g58: طلایه is the subject 'the vanguard/forefront'. بیامد is past 'came' (classical form). به پیش is a prepositional phrase 'in front of/before' and takes سپاه ('army, host') as its object. The structure is Subject + Verb + prepositional phrase indicating position relative to the army.
segments.s217-g59: گشادند is past plural 'they loosened/spread' used here as an active verb. گردان is the subject 'troops/squadrons'. سراسر is an adverbial 'throughout/entire' modifying the following noun کمر, which here means 'belt/girdle' (i.e. their waists/belts). The phrase describes the troops loosening belts over the whole waist/line.
segments.s217-g60: بخوان and بخوردن are infinitival/verbal nouns 'to sing/chant' and 'to eat' used as activities; و is the conjunction 'and'. نهادند is past 'they put/they set' used here idiomatically to mean 'they set about' or 'they began'. سر ('head') here helps mark the direct object or the thing they set (i.e. they laid down to, or applied themselves to, singing and eating) — overall idiomatically 'they began to sing and to eat.'
segments.s217-g61: بلشکر is a compound adverbial 'with the army' (literally 'in/with the host'). گه is an adverb meaning 'on that occasion' or 'then'. آمد is past 'came'. سپهدار is the title 'commander' and طوس is the proper name Tūs. Word order (adverbial phrase + temporal adverb + verb + title + name) is typical of classical narrative.
segments.s217-g62: پر از is the adjectival phrase 'full of'. خون دل is a poetic collocation ('blood of the heart') meaning literally bleeding/with blood (or figuratively grief/agony). و is 'and'. روی is 'face'. چون is the simile marker 'like' and سندروس is a fragrant wood (sandalwood) used metaphorically for appearance. The whole phrase describes a bloodied/anguished heart and a face compared to sandalwood.
segments.s217-g63: بگودرز is the narrative label 'Goudarz' (here indicating the speaker). گفت is the past verb 'said' (note: the provided sense list did not include 'say', so sense is marked -1). این سخن = 'this speech/these words'. تیره گشت = 'became dark/ominous' where تیره is used figuratively 'ominous' and گشت is past 'became/turned'. The clause reports Goudarz's remark that the words portend ill.
segments.s217-g64: سر بخت is a genitive phrase 'the head/prime of fortune' i.e. 'the fortune' or 'the lot'; ایرانیان is the possessor 'of the Iranians'. خیره گشت is a compound predicate 'became aghast/was struck dumb' where خیره = 'astonished' and گشت = 'became'. The structure is Possessed + possessor + predicate (the fate/fortune of the Iranians became astonished/awful).
segments.s217-g81: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. خورشید is the subject 'the sun'. برزد is an archaic intransitive past/preterite-like verb here meaning 'came/fell forth, broke forth' (older usage). ز marks source 'from', and خرچنگ چنگ is a genitival phrase 'the crab's claw' (خرچنگ = crab; چنگ = claw) giving the point of emergence.
segments.s217-g82: بدرید is a past verb 'tore' (active). پیراهن مشک رنگ is a noun phrase where پیراهن (robe/outer garment) is modified postpositively by the compound adjective مشک رنگ 'musk-colored' (مشک = musk; رنگ = color). The object is unmarked by را, as common in Persian.
segments.s217-g83: به پیران is the dative/indirect object 'to the elders'. فرستاده is a noun 'a messenger' functioning as the subject; آمد is the past-tense verb 'came'. ز شاه indicates the source 'from the king'. Word order (dative – subject – verb – source) is typical in classical Persian narrative.
segments.s217-g84: که introduces the reported content / subordinate clause 'that/which'. آمد ز هر جای marks that something 'came from every place' (آمد = came), where هر جای = 'every quarter/place'. بی‌مر is an archaic/uncertain qualifier attached to سپاه (here to be read as modifying 'army'—likely 'countless' or similar); سپاه is the noun 'army/host' and serves as the subject of the embedded clause.
segments.s217-g85: سپاهی is the noun 'an army'. که begins a relative clause 'which'. دریای چین را is the definite object phrase 'the Sea of China' (دریای = 'sea of', چین = here the place-name 'China'), and را is the object marker. ز گرد uses گرد in the sense 'dust' (ز گرد = 'by/from its dust'), indicating the means or accompaniment by which the army acts.
segments.s217-g86: کند is the present/habitual 'makes/does' (3rd-person). چون = 'like/as', introducing a similative complement; بیابان is 'desert'. بروز here means 'on the day/at the time (of)', and نبرد is the noun 'battle' — together بروز نبرد = 'on the day of battle'. Grammatically the clause says the army 'makes (the sea) like a desert on the day of battle.'
segments.s217-g87: نخستین is the ordinal adjective 'first/foremost' modifying سپهدار 'commander'. خاقان چین is a genitival phrase 'the khagan of China' modifying سپهدار (i.e. 'the khagan of China’s commander' or 'the commander of the Chinese khagan'). Word order places the modifiers before the head noun in this phrase.
segments.s217-g88: که introduces a relative clause 'who/whose'. تختش is تخت + possessive -ش 'his throne'. همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle 'indeed/still' often used in classical Persian. برنتابد is an older/poetic verb form (listed as obscure) here understood as 'does not bear/endure' (so the sense is 'the earth cannot bear his throne'), and زمین is the object 'the earth'. The whole relative clause describes the extraordinary nature of his throne.
segments.s217-g257: بسی is a pre-nominal quantifier ('many/much') placed before the noun; تخم نیکی is a noun+noun compound meaning 'seed of goodness' (تخم modifies نیکی). پراگنده‌ایم is a predicative form with the 1st-person-plural ending -ایم; poetically it functions as a perfect/stative predicate 'we have spread/scattered' or 'we are scattered/widespread' and links back to تخم نیکی as its object/theme.
segments.s217-g258: جهان‌آفرین is the definite referent 'the world‑creator/Creator'; را is the accusative/definite-object marker placed after that noun for emphasis/topicalization; پرستنده‌ایم is a nominal predicate with the copular -ایم ('we are worshippers/devotees of…'), so the whole segment means 'we are worshippers of the Creator (of the world)'.
segments.s217-g259: و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'; دیگر here functions adverbially ('furthermore/again' or 'another') introducing a new item; ببخت is a preposed adjective 'ill‑fated/unfortunate' modifying the following titles; جهاندار شاه is a stacked title ('world‑ruler, king') with the modifiers before the head noun, typical of poetic apposition.
segments.s217-g260: خداوند here is an honorific/sovereign sense 'lord' (not necessarily the theological God in this line) introducing a list of regal attributes/possessions; the nouns شمشیر و تخت و کلاه are coordinated by و ('sword and throne and crown/helmet') and follow the head without a genitive marker — a common poetic juxtaposition meaning 'lord/master of sword, throne and crown.'
segments.s217-g261: ندارد is the negative 3rd‑person present of داشتن used here ('does not have' / 'is not without'); the verb appears before the subject (poetic inversion) and governs the verbal phrase دست یاز, a compound meaning 'to reach out/lay hands on' or 'to stretch forth a hand' (i.e., to intervene or help).
segments.s217-g262: که introduces a subordinate clause with temporal/conditional force ('that/when/if'); آید is 3rd‑person singular 'comes' (present/subjunctive flavor in poetry); ببدخواه is the noun 'ill‑wisher/enemy' as the logical subject of آید; ما را is the clitic object 'to/for us' (را marks it as definite/emphatic); نیاز is the noun 'need/hardship/supplication' — together the clause reads roughly 'when an ill‑wisher comes, (our) need (hardship) [befalls/is present].' Grammatically the subordinate clause gives the condition under which the Creator 'stretches out a hand.'
segments.s217-g263: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'; رستم is the proper name subject; بیاید (poetic present/subjunctive) 'comes' indicates the arrival; بدین is a contracted prepositional phrase (به + این) 'to this' and precedes the locative noun رزمگاه 'battlefield/place of contest'. The whole segment means 'when Rostam comes to this battlefield.'
segments.s217-g264: بدیها is the plural subject 'evils/wickednesses'; سرآید is a poetic 3rd‑person verb that can mean 'come/arrive' or 'come to an end' — here it is best read as 'cease/end' given the context; همه 'all/everyone' and بر سپاه 'upon the army/host' specify the scope/target. Word order is inverted for poetic effect (verb before subject), yielding 'all evils will come to an end for the host.'
segments.s217-g89: تنش is تن + enclitic possessive -ش (“his body”), so the whole noun phrase is the subject. زور دارد is a present simple possession construction (دارد = 'has'); چو introduces a simile ('like/as'), followed by the comparison phrase صد نره شیر ('a hundred male lions'). Word order is Subject – Verb – simile; چو governs the following nominal phrase.
segments.s217-g90: This clause expresses the thing made to move: سر ژنده پیل = 'the (elephant's) head (ژنده here adjectival/'ragged' or descriptive)'; Persian often places modifiers before/after nouns poetically. اندر آرد بزیر is a verb phrase: آرد (3sg present of آوردن, 'brings/causes to bring') with the locative/into marker اندر and the directional adverb بزیر ('downwards/to the ground'). The verb comes after its objects/phrases in this poetic order.
segments.s217-g91: یکی here is an indefinite 'a certain' introducing an individual; مهتر is the noun 'chief/headman'. از ماورالنهر is a prepositional phrase 'from Māwar al-Nahr (Transoxiana)'. The postposed particle بر functions adverbially ('up/on/aloft') and connects to the following verb phrase; in classical poetic syntax such directional/adverbial particles often appear after the noun phrase they modify.
segments.s217-g92: که introduces a relative clause modifying the preceding 'one chief' ('who/that'). بگذارد is the 3rd-person form of گذارد (here 'would place / set'); in narrative it expresses habitual or characteristic action. از + چرخ گردنده = 'beyond/from the revolving wheel' (here چرخ گردنده is a poetic image of the turning wheel/heaven/fate) and سر is the direct object (head) placed or set beyond it. Note the poetic order: preposition phrase + modifier + object + verb.
segments.s217-g93: ببالا is an adverbial phrase 'on high/aloft'; چو introduces the simile 'like', followed by سرو ('cypress'). The conjunction و links to بدیدار ماه, where بدیدار functions poetically to mean 'in appearance/visage' or 'in sight/meeting' and ماه is 'the moon' (used as a beauty-contrast). The whole segment gives two parallel similes describing loftiness and beauty.
segments.s217-g94: This short clause is condensed/elliptical: جهانگیر and نازان are coordinate descriptors (here 'world‑conquering' / a title and 'coquettish' / 'beloved'); بدو = 'to him' (dative). تاج و گاه are two nouns 'crown and (place/throne)' serving as the objects given to him; the verb ('gave/placed') is omitted for poetic brevity but implied. Note that در/بدو marks the beneficiary ('unto him').
segments.s217-g95: سر سرافرازان is a genitival/nominal phrase 'the heads of the exalted/honored ones' (سرافرازان is plural honorific). The coordinated phrase و کاموس نام reads as an appositional or nominal phrase where a proper name (کاموس) is followed by نام ('named/so-called'); in poetry 'X نام' often means 'called X'. The whole segment lists persons/titles exalted or named.
segments.s217-g96: برآرد is 3sg present of برآوردن ('brings forth/raises/causes to appear'). ز (poetic form of از) marks source 'from Goudarz' and از طوس repeats 'and from Tūs'; نام at the end functions as the thing brought forth — 'name' or 'fame' (i.e., he raised their names/fame). The clause is therefore 'he brought forth the name/fame from Goudarz and from Tūs.'
segments.s217-g241: که here is a subordinating conjunction introducing the clause ('that' / marking the reported fact). امروز is an adverb of time ('today/now'). ترکان is the plural subject 'the Turks'. چرا is the interrogative 'why' placed before the verb. خامش‌اند is an archaic/poetic predicative: خامش (variant of خاموش) + اند (third-person plural copula), meaning 'they are silent' or 'they hold their peace'. Word order places the verb at the end as typical in Persian poetry.
segments.s217-g242: برای + بداند functions as a purpose/infinitival-like phrase: 'in order to know' or 'to find out' (برای = 'in order to', بداند = infinitive/present form of دانستن 'to know/learn'). ار is the conditional 'if'. ز is the classical/prepositional 'from' or 'because of'. می‌بیهش‌اند is an irregular/obscure classical verbal form with the progressive/iterative prefix می- and plural ending -اند; its root is rare here and meaning uncertain in modern Persian, so the clause reads 'if from X they are ...' — i.e. 'to see whether they are so because of (wine)'.
segments.s217-g243: اگر begins a conditional 'if'. مستمندند‌گر is a fused/classical expression: مستمند (needy/poor) with an attached conditional/contrastive گر (here functioning like 'or if' / 'and if'); taken together it presents an alternative condition 'if they are needy' (the form reflects older poetic compounding). شادمان is an adjective 'joyful/cheerful'.
segments.s217-g244: شدم is the past tense 'I became' (perfective of شدن). در گمان is a prepositional phrase 'in thought / in suspicion' (در = 'in', گمان = 'suspicion/guess'). از بد بدگمان stacks prepositional + adjective + noun: از ('from/of'), بد ('bad/ill'), بدگمان ('suspicious' / 'one who suspects'); together giving the sense 'I was seized by a bad suspicion' or 'I began to suspect the worst.'
segments.s217-g245: اگرشان is the conditional 'if' with a third‑person enclitic sense ('for them' / 'to them') fused in classical style; here it introduces the condition. به پیکار = 'to/into battle' (به + پیکار). یار is 'companion/ally' (object or subject depending on reading). آمدست is an archaic perfect/present-perfect 'has come' (آمد + است contracted to آمدست), indicating completion.
segments.s217-g246: چنان دان is an imperative or hortative-like formula 'consider it so / think thus' (چنان = 'so', دان here is cognate with دانستن used as 'consider/regard'). که introduces the content clause 'that ...'. بد روزگار آمدست uses بد روزگار = 'ill fate/bad times' and آمدست as above 'has come', i.e. 'that ill fortune has arrived.'
segments.s217-g247: تو is the vocative/imperative addressee 'you'. ایرانیان را is the definite object 'the Iranians' (را marks the definite object). همه 'all/everyone' modifies ایرانیان. کشته is the adjective/past participle 'killed/slain'. گیر is an imperative or command 'take/seize' (classical poetic imperative form of گرفتن), so the clause reads as an order about the Iranians ('you, take all the Iranians slain').
segments.s217-g248: وگر is a conditional 'but if / and if'. زنده 'alive'. از رزم برگشته is a relative/time phrase 'returned from the fight' (از = 'from', رزم = 'battle', برگشته = 'returned'). گیر again is the imperative 'seize/capture'. The whole second half gives the alternative order: 'and if any have come back alive from the battle, seize them.'
segments.s217-g177: A coordination of nouns joined by و (and): زن (woman), کودک خرد (literally 'child small' = 'little child'), پیر (old/elderly) و جوان (and young). Word order here places the modifying adjective خرد after کودک (a common poetic order), and the repeated و links the enumerated groups.
segments.s217-g178: نمانم is the first-person form of ماندن used negatively/volitively here: 'I will not let remain / I will not leave (any)'. که introduces a relative/purpose clause ('who/that'), and باشد is the subjunctive/optative third-person form of بودن ('may/should be' or simply 'is' in classical style). تنی با روان is a compact noun phrase 'a body with (a) soul' = 'an animate person'; together the clause means 'so that no body with a soul remains' (i.e. none left alive).
segments.s217-g179: بر و بوم ایران is a paired nominal phrase meaning 'the land(s) and dwellings of Iran' (بر and بوم paired to mean territory). It appears before the verb as the theme, and نمانم repeats the speaker's volitive 'I will leave none (remaining)'. بجای is an adverbial 'in place / remaining' modifying نمانم: 'I will leave none in place'.
segments.s217-g180: که introduces a wish clause ('so that / that'), and مه here is an archaic/formal first‑person element (used by Ferdowsi as 'I' / 'my'); since that pronoun sense is not in the supplied sense list it is marked -1. دست بادا is an optative formula: دست (hand) + بادا ('may it be') = 'may my hand be...'. ازیشان (ازِ ایشان) functions here as 'upon/against them' (from/of them in poetic usage). The second مه is again the archaic personal element 'my' (marked -1). پای (foot) completes the parallel wish: 'may my hand (and) my foot be on them.'
segments.s217-g181: کنون 'now' sets the temporal frame. تا introduces the subordinate clause 'until', followed by the first-person subjunctive کنم ('that I do / I accomplish'), so تا کنم کارها را بسیچ = 'until I have done many deeds'. کارها is the direct object marked by را, and بسیچ (a poetic intensifier) quantifies it as 'many' / 'much'.
segments.s217-g182: This is an imperative/advisory prohibition addressed to شما (you, plural). جنگ ایشان is 'war against them' (object phrase placed before the verb, common in Persian). مجویید is the negative present/imperative form (you do not/should not seek), and هیچ is an emphatic negator 'at all / not in the least' — together: 'you must not seek war with them at all.'
segments.s217-g183: بفگت is an archaic narrative past 'he said' (classical form of گفت). این و links 'he said this and ...'. دل پر ز کینه is a noun phrase 'heart full of hatred' (پر 'full' follows the noun; ز introduces the source/quality 'with/from hatred'). برفت is the past of رفتن 'he went away/left' — here a simple past narrative verb. The clause describes his departure with a heart full of grudge.
segments.s217-g184: همی is a narrative/emphatic particle (here linking the following statement). پوست بر تنش is a genitive/locative phrase 'the skin on his body' with the enclitic -ش marking 'his'. گفتی here functions idiomatically with بکفت ('was enough') — together the phrase means 'the skin (i.e. his appearance) said/sufficed; it said enough' (i.e. his look spoke volumes). بکفت is a classical past-of-sufficiency 'was enough'.
